items.title: Python script for automating the extraction of document links from a comapany network and writing to an Excel file
items.text: I am in the process of finalizing a database in XLSX format containing all the products of my company where I am an intern. In particular, I created a column where I insert one by one the links leading to the user sheets as well as the technical sheets of these products. These links will be read and processed by a local AI model that I call later in my Python pipeline in order to process the semantic similarity between each document. There are 330 products and doing this manually is very time-consuming. So I tried to create a Python extraction script using ChatGPT and Gemini. I methodically described to them what I was doing manually, step by step, for a given product so that they could offer me an extraction script. Here are the prompts given to GPT and Gemini: "CONTEXTE: Je veux insérer des liens présents sur le réseau de mon entreprise dans la feuille "master_file" de mon classeur "product_catalog_V8_links" ci-joint. Les liens menant aux fichiers PDF contenant des notices d'utilisation sont placés dans la colonne M ("instructions_for_use"), tandis que les liens menant aux dossiers de validation techniques sont palcés dans la colonne N ("Validation_file").. La logique que je suis manuellement est la suivante: 1. j'accède au réseau via le lien “\\dc2022adia\BioxAdia\R11_Marketing\0_Library” 2. je vais chercher dans la colonne A de la feuille “master_file” la référence produit recherchée; elle est en début de cellule entre crochets. (ex: “[ADI631-100] ADIAVET SALMONELLA ST/SE FAST TIME 100R” en colonne A86) (cf. screenshot “1” en PJ) 3. je tape le code produit dans la barre de recherche en haut à droite en réduisant le code produit à sa “base”, sans le conditionnement (ex:ADI631-100 devient ADI631) 4. j’obtiens ensuite : a. soit une liste de fichiers et un dossier parent de ces fichiers nommé explicitement “ADI631”, b. soit uniquement des fichiers (cas minoritaire); 5. je clique sur le dossier (cas a.) s’il apparaît (cf. screenshot “2” en PJ). J’ouvre ensuite les dossiers enfants “Dossier de validation” et “Notices” dans un nouvel onglet avec un clic droit. 6. Dans “Notices”, je cherche un fichier répondant à 2 critères: a. en anglais (obligatoire). Ces fichiers sont indiqués avec les critères suivants: i. (EN) écrit vers la fin du nom du fichier, avant le numéro de version (exemple: ADC22LD-CSFV-NO-(EN)-V01.pdf ) ii. NE écrit en début de fichier (ex: NE014S-03.pdf) b. version la plus récente (si disponible). Le numéro de version est indiqué de la façon suivante: i. V0x en fin de fichier (ex: ADC72LD-EHDV-NO-(EN)-V02.pdf) ii. -xx.pdf en fin de fichier (ex:NE601-01.pdf) iii. soit il n’y a aucune indication donc ce critère saute . 7. 2 cas de figure: a. je ne trouve pas de fichier répondant aux critères 6. C’est le cas ici (cf. screenshot “3”) Je constate ici qu’il n’y a aucun fichier répondant au critère 6)a. Ma recherche s’arrête alors pour la notice de la cellule / produit A86 et je marque en rouge (couleur de remplissage) la cellule “instructions_for_use” relative à la cellule / produit A86 sur laquelle on travaille, soit la cellule M86 (cf. 7.a dans le screenhot “4”). b. je trouve un fichier répondant aux critères 6. Imaginons ici que le fichier “ADI631-STSE-DV-(FR)-V01” me donne satisfaction. Alors je fais clic droit dessus >propriétés > sécurité. Je copie ensuite le “nom de l’objet” (cf. screenshot “4”) que j’insère en lien hypertexte dans la cellule “instructions_for_use” relative à la cellule / produit A86 sur laquelle on travaille, soit la cellule M86 (cf. 7.b dans le screenshot 4). 8. Je passe ensuite à l’onglet “Dossier de validation” de la cellule / produit A86 pour répéter exactement la même procédure que pour l’onglet “notices” dans le cas de figure où le dossier de la cellule / produit A86 contenait bien un dossier nommé ““Dossier de validation” . Il faut noter les différences suivantes: a. On insérera les liens trouvés en colonne N de la feuille “master_file”, contrairement aux liens “instructions_for_use” qui étaient inscrits en colonne M. b. écriture en anglais: i. -en en fin de fichier (ex: R262-C-en.pdf ) ii. (EN) écrit vers la fin du nom du fichier, avant le numéro de version (exemple: ADI045-PTUB-DV-(EN)-V01.pdf) Dans le cas de figure où le dossier de la cellule / produit A86 ne contenait pas de dossier nommé ““Dossier de validation”, on passe à l’étape 9. 9. Une fois l’étape 8. achevé, je passe à la la cellule / produit A87, etc… jusqu’à la dernière cellule / produit de la colonne A (A338). Si pour un produit donné, tu n'as obtenu que des fichiers à l’étape 4 (cas minoritaire), inscrit marque en violet (couleur de remplissage) la cellule “instructions_for_use” sur le modèle de l’étape 7.a, puis passe au produit suivant (cf étape 9). OBJECTIF: Propose moi un script python pour automatiser entièrement cette procédure" Here is what GPT Gave me: import re from pathlib import Path from collections import defaultdict from openpyxl import load_workbook from openpyxl.styles import PatternFill

items.title: Magento 2 checkout does not enable map in Cypress blur/enter events
items.text: I am automating Magento 2 checkout with Cypress and am experiencing a synchronization issue with Knockout related to a Google Maps map/autocomplete in the address form (shipping). In Magento 2 checkout, the shipping form is built with Knockout, and the map/autocomplete component is only enabled when an internal observable (e.g., shouldShowAddressForm) changes to true. That change depends on form validation events (blur/enter/click outside). The problem is that in E2E execution with Cypress, even though I: do type() on the inputs, trigger {enter}, trigger blur() and click outside the form, the map is not enabled before Cypress continues to the next step in the flow. Therefore, the map/autocomplete remains “disabled” or does not initialize, and the test fails (or proceeds without the map validating/completing the address). Expected behavior After filling in the address fields (street/city/postcode, etc.) and triggering validation events, the observable shouldShowAddressForm should change to true and the map/autocomplete should initialize or become enabled. Current behavior In Cypress, the observable does not change before the next step of the test. The map is not enabled, and even a reload does not fix it, because the base condition (shouldShowAddressForm === true) never occurs during the test flow. What I have already tried Triggering validation events manually: if (email) { this.elements.emailInput() .clear() .type(email, { delay: 50 }) .blur(); // Breve pausa para validación AJAX de email (verificar si existe cuenta) cy.wait(300); } if (firstName) { this.elements.firstNameInput() .clear() .type(firstName, { delay: 50 }) .blur(); } if (lastName) { this.elements.lastNameInput() .clear() .type(lastName, { delay: 50 }) .blur(); } if (phone) { this.elements.phoneInput() .clear() .type(phone, { delay: 50 }) .blur(); } if (documentType) { this.elements.documentTypeSelect() .select(documentType) .blur(); } if (documentId) { this.elements.documentIdInput() .clear() .type(documentId, { delay: 50 }) .blur(); }

items.title: Newman API tests failing with Cloudflare Access headers
items.text: I'm running Newman API tests in a GitHub Actions workflow that needs to pass Cloudflare Access headers. The workflow is failing even though I believe I've implemented everything Postman Collection has requests with CF Access headers: { "key": "CF-Access-Client-Id", "value": "{{CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID}}" }, { "key": "CF-Access-Client-Secret", "value": "{{CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET}}" } Environment File has the variables defined: { "key": "CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID", "value": "", "type": "secret", "enabled": true }, { "key": "CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET", "value": "", "type": "secret", "enabled": true } - name: Update environment file... cf_access_client_id = os.environ.get('CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID', '') cf_access_client_secret = os.environ.get('CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET', '') with open('newman/environment.json', 'r') as f: env_data = json.load(f) for item in env_data.get('values', []): key = item.get('key') if key == 'CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID': item['value'] = cf_access_client_id elif key == 'CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET': item['value'] = cf_access_client_secret with open(env_file, 'w') as f: json.dump(env_data, f, indent=2) EOF env: CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID: ${{ secrets.CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID }} CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET }} when validating the collection the workflow is failing as it is hitting the CF access page (html error) so seems like the CF are not being read correctly

items.title: csh script calling some .sql scripts but not others
items.text: I have a csh script set up to run multiple .sql scripts within SQLPLUS. It is running some scripts, but not others. The scripts all work as expected when run individually in SQLPLUS, so I know the problem isn't with the scripts themselves. This is my csh script: #!/bin/csh -f set pw = [password] sqlplus -s /nolog << EOF connect [username]/${pw} @ /path/to/script/script1.sql input1 input2 @ /path/to/script/script2.sql input1 input2 @ /path/to/script/script3.sql input1 input2 @ /path/to/script/script4.sql input1 input2 @ /path/to/script/script5.sql input1 input2 @ /path/to/script/script6.sql input1 input2 EOF When I run the csh script, it creates output files for scripts 1, 3, 5 and 6. Scripts 2 and 4 appear to not run at all. They do not create any output, nor do they appear in the stdout log. Addition: Even stranger, if I comment out scripts 1, 3, 5, and 6, then scripts 2 and 4 do run. Any ideas why this would happen, and how to fix it? I am running this on a Solaris 11.4

items.title: Delete a section of a file Bash Script
items.text: I’m trying to remove 3 lines of code in a file so I thought at first to use sed and rm lines 1-3 like this: sed ‘1d;2d;3d’ file but then my lead system admin said I may run into issues because in another scenario there might be more code so that wouldn’t work. So then I thought maybe to match the lines like this: sed ‘/specific thingy/d’ file-path sed ‘/next thingy/d’ file-path sed ‘/last line/d’ file-path But then he said “what if the lines are different” and it dosent exactly match so then he gave me a hint to use grep. In the actual code the first and third line are comments so how would you grep the first comment, last comment, and then anything inbetween those comments delete?

items.title: Is a fish shell variable just a string separated with spaces?
items.text: The docs say that all variables are lists, and I thought that meant they had some special internal structure that was outside the scope of the shell interface. Like, how the , in the python list [1,2,3] is just part of the representation of the list, not part of the actual list, and you can't change the delimiter to something else. But I did some tests: $ set -l a aa ab ac ad $ echo $a[2] ab $ set a (echo $a | sed 's/a/z/g') $ echo $a zz zb zc zd $ set a (echo $a | set 's/ /,/g') $ echo $a zz,zb,zc,zd So, I can just modify the entire variable like it is just a normal string. It seems to me that a variable is just a string, and the spaces are interpreted as delimiters between elements.

items.title: Why is the output of the function getTransformedField() different from that queried in Abaqus/Viewer?
items.text: I am writing an Abaqus/Python script that transforms FieldOutput values of a large deformation problem from the global cartesian to a user-defined moving cylindrical coordinate system. Since I am new to Abaqus/Python scripting, I cross checked the values the code outputted against the node query output in Viewer. I get similar values, but not exactly the same values ( screenshot ). In Viewer I use the Transformation tab under Result Option dropdown ( image attached ). Below is my Python script rootAssy = odb.rootAssembly instance1 = odb.rootAssembly.instances['PART-1-1'] step = odb.steps['Interference'] lastFrame = step.frames[-1] nset = instance1.nodeSets['CS_INNER_S'] CSYSnode1 = instance1.nodeSets['NSET_A'].nodes[-1] CSYSnode2 = instance1.nodeSets['NSET_B'].nodes[-1] CSYSnode3 = instance1.nodeSets['NSET_C'].nodes[-1] cylCSYS = rootAssy.DatumCsysByThreeCircNodes(name='cylCSYS', coordSysType=CYLINDRICAL, node1Arc=CSYSnode1, node2Arc=CSYSnode2, node3Arc=CSYSnode3) disp = lastFrame.fieldOutputs['U'] COORD_fieldOutput = lastFrame.fieldOutputs['COORD'] transformedCOORD = COORD_fieldOutput.getTransformedField(datumCsys=cylCSYS, deformationField=disp) transformedCOORD_nset = transformedCOORD.getSubset(region=nset, position=NODAL) transformedScalarCOORD1 = transformedCOORD_nset.getScalarField(componentLabel='COOR1').values[0] transformedScalarCOORD2 = transformedCOORD_nset.getScalarField(componentLabel='COOR2').values[0] transformedScalarCOORD3 = transformedCOORD_nset.getScalarField(componentLabel='COOR3').values[0] print('Node: ', transformedScalarCOORD1.nodeLabel) print('COOR1: ',transformedCOORD_nset.values[0].data[0], ', COOR2: ',transformedCOORD_nset.values[0].data[1], ', COOR3: ',transformedCOORD_nset.values[0].data[2]) Am I missing something? In addition, I also see that the COOR2 contour legend has a range of approx. (-45,45) degrees, This seems a wrong to me, I was expecting range of (-90,90) degrees. Here is a screenshot. It would be much appreciated if someone could give me some pointers.

items.title: return value from a vimscript to a variable in calling bash script
items.text:
First off, allow me to apologize for the confusion this question has created, and for my tardiness in responding. I live completely off-grid, and can only access internet at the library when I come to town. Second, I spent yesterday finding the solution to the problem, which is simply that the vim line below should be -S rather than -u. Vim help recommended the use of -u because it allows vim messages which -S suppresses. Sourcing the script in vim's window with -u worked fine, but when called from my bash script, those messages interfered with the much faster execution of the vimscript so that many of its commands failed. -S suppressed those messages, and the execution of the script was successful. In a bash script I run vim headless with vim $i.txt -u $HDV/test.vim " -u should be -S if vim headless where test.vim terminates with call writefile([@n.' '.@m], "tr.cnt") q! Third, the above would seem to eliminate the need for the question, but my original reason for it (at the time believing I was getting the result written to file), was to discover how to deliver the result to a variable accessible by the calling bash script. In other words, something different in place of the 'call writefile...' line above. I would still appreciate pointers to doing that, if possible. I had tried assigning the vim call above to a variable: trct=$(vim $i.txt...) which failed, and I also followed a recommendation on a webpage to export it to a variable defined in my .bashrc, which also was unsuccessful. And lastly, to fulfil the conditions required for reinstating the question, following are the bash and vim scripts concerned,and 2 jpgs which should be in in a directory d inside the pwd. The vimscript should be named test.vim " counts of trash characters generated by tesseract, and bad words %s/\(\a\)\- *
\(\a\)/\1\2/e " remove line end hyphens %s/`\(\a\)/'\1/ge " speech shwn only by '...' or "..." %s/[[:punct:]-]\+/ /ge " remove punctuation and hyphens %s/\(\a\) \(d\|ll\|m\|re\|s\|t\|ve\)\>/\1'\2/ge " restore apostrophes %s/\<\(o\) \(clock\)/\1'\2/ge " ditto %s/[(){}\[\]*$%\-]\+/ /ge " remove acceptable non-word characters %s/\s\+/ /ge " single space throughout %s/
\+/ /e " all words on 1 line let @l=col("$") " number of characters in file s/\W/ /ge " remove all trash characters s/\(\a\) \(d\|ll\|m\|re\|s\|t\|ve\)\>/\1'\2/ge " restore apostrophes s/\<\(o\) \(clock\)/\1'\2/ge " restore apostrophes s/\s\+/ /ge " single space throughout let @m=col("$") " characters in file after trash removed let @n=@l-@m " @n is number of trash characters s/\s\+//ge " each word on its own line sort u " each word unique %s/
\+/ /e " and all on one line if @n==0 let @n=1 " prevents division by zero endif let @m=0 set nows setl spell spl=en wh spellbadword[1]=='bad' let @m=@m+1 " counts the bad words once only each norm El endwh if @m==0 let @m=1 " prevents division by zero endif call writefile([@n.' '.@m], "tr.cnt") q! " restore file to original state and the bash # OCR texts from 1 2 4 8 16 times size of images # any $1 shuts system down at eoj, interrupt by >~/break.er mkdir a b c 2> /dev/null r='100 200 400 800 1600' fls=($r) >tr.cnt ctr=0 for a in d/*.*; do let ctr=ctr+1 trm $a dia='' wds='' j=0 echo $a $(date +%T)|tee -a ocr.log # ocr current image at each %age magnification in $r for i in $r; do echo "${a:2:5}x$i" > $i.txt echo "$i.txt" >> ocr.log # 1600% resize only useful if other sizes produced no text if [ $i -eq 1600 ] && [ $j -gt 0 ]; then break; fi if [ $i -gt 100 ]; then convert $a -resize $i% -normalize - | tesseract - q 2>> ocr.log else tesseract $a q 2>> ocr.log fi uni2ascii -Bq q.txt >> $i.txt # compute words/(diacritics*reds) ratio for current .txt vim $i.txt -S $HDV/test.vim # red word count of current .txt until [ -f tr.cnt ]; do sleep 1; done trct=($(cat tr.cnt)) wds=($(wc -w $i.txt)) # number of words produced by tesseract if [ ${wds[0]} -eq 0 ]; then ratio[$j]=0 let j=j+1 continue else # get appropriate number of diacritics from tesseract dcs=($(grep diacritics <<< $(tail -1 ocr.log) ) ) # why blue???? if grep "Empty" <<< ${dcs[@]}; then dia=999; fi if [[ ${dcs[0]} == '' ]]; then dia=1; else dia=${dcs[1]}; fi fi # calculate words/(diacritics*reds*trash) ratio for current .txt let ratio[j]=1000*wds[0]/\(dia*trct[0]*trct[1]\) let j=j+1 done # accept the .txt whose ratio (above) is greatest max=0 for ((j=0; j<4; j++)); do if [ ${ratio[$j]} -gt $max ]; then max=${ratio[$j]} k=$j echo "max = $max in ${fls[$k]}.txt" >> ocr.log fi done cat ${fls[$k]}.txt >> y # .txt with largest ratio added to final text y # mv t.png c/${a:2} # trimmed image to c/ # mv $a b/ # original to b/ if [ -f ~/break.er ]; then break; fi # quit if user interrupt if (( $(acpi | grep -o '[0-9]\+%'|grep -o '[0-9]\+') < 4 )); then xdotool key Super_L+q # shut system down if battery charge < 4% break 2 fi done # rm q.txt ~/break.er 2> /dev/null if [[ "$1" != "" ]]; then xdotool key Super_L+q; fi Besides vim, the bash calls for tesseract, uni2ascii imagema

items.title: How can I loop through a JSON file using bash and extract values to variables?
items.text: I have a json file, vars.json, of arbitrary length, in a format like [ { "environment1": { "variable1": "Value1", "variable2": "Value2" }, "environment2": { "variable1": "Value3", "variable2": "Value4" } } ] I would like to loop through this data using a bash script, and extract the name of the environment (eg "environment1") and the value of variable1 (eg "value1") for each environment as bash variables, to use later in the loop. I know I can use jq to get the value of Variable1 for a single environment jq -r '(.[] | [.environment1.variable1]) ' vars.json or use [] to get all values for variable 1 across all environments, but I'm not certain how best to get exactly what I need. I envision my final script looking something like this pseudocode: #!/bin/bash for each key in vars.json do environment_name=[some jq that gets the key] variable1_value=[some jq that gets the value of variable 1] variable2_value=[some jq that gets the value of variable 2] echo "for $environment_name the first value is $variable1_value and the second value is $variable2_value" done with an output that reads like for environment1 the first value is Value1 and the second value is Value2 for environment2 the first value is Value3 and the second value is Value4 I hope this all makes sense. Thank you for your help

items.title: How should I interpret SELECT vs INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE ratios when designing indexes for an OLTP workload?
items.text: I'm analyzing a SQL Server 2022 OLTP database and trying to refine my indexing strategy based on actual workload patterns. The exact schema and queries cannot be shared, but the observed ratios and workload patterns are accurate. I collected these numbers from Query Store, by grouping captured statements from sys.query_store_query_text and their execution counts from sys.query_store_runtime_stats . Using Query Store / DMVs, I observed the following distribution of operations: * SELECT: ~53% * INSERT: ~20% * UPDATE: ~25% * DELETE: ~5% Context: * Application: transactional (tasks/projects management) * Tables: moderate size for now, but expected to grow significantly * Workload: mixed read/write (roughly 50/50) * Current concern: balancing read performance vs write overhead due to non-clustered indexes I understand that indexing improves reads but adds overhead to writes, so I'm trying to make an informed trade-off. My questions: 1. Is there a practical way to interpret such ratios when deciding how aggressively to index? 2. Are there commonly used heuristics to classify a workload as read-heavy vs write-heavy? 3. In a roughly balanced workload (~50/50), what are the typical indexing strategies in practice? * Fewer, more generic indexes? * Or more targeted indexes despite write cost? 4. Do experienced practitioners rely on such ratios, or is query-level analysis always preferred over global metrics? I'm not looking for a purely theoretical answer — practical rules of thumb or real-world experience would be very helpful.

The Messy Desktop Files Problem A Mac-like launcher for Windows users. Supports PowerShell command execution, global file searching, and searching for saved macros in the search bar. (Macros save file information. Here's an example structure:) { name: "name to render in file list", path: "actual path (for open)", openBy: "software you want to use when opening. Default is 'default'. Depends on your system default", icon: "icon for showing in file list" } 2. The Data Transfer Problem Checkpoint support (resume broken transfers). Encrypted transfers. Cross-platform (Windows/macOS/Linux/Android/iOS). Server-simulated transfer with background support. Supports text/voice messages, audio calls, and file transfers. I'm going to use Go for the backend and TypeScript for the frontend. Maybe I'll use Wasm for complex calculation tasks. Which problem would you solve first? Is anyone interested in this or has similar problems? Thanks for reading, everyone! Let me know your priority.

items.title: Best way to manage a full reindex while allowing collections to work
items.text: We have a job that performs a full reindex of all our indices (30) every day, with the following flow for each index: Copy reference index x settings to x_tmp Perform 5 parallel calls to the pushByIndexName service ( Injestion API, called directly, no client, because it's orchestrated by Mulesoft) to add 40 objects (this allows staying in the 5MB payload) - For a total of ~1200 objects I counted ~60 calls Poll on each response task id, to check the status When all the tasks are completed, work the following index. This procedure allows the collections connector to work automatically as soon as you push data, to keep everything aligned for Merchandise Studio. However, these indices tend to be a bit massive (since each record contains a small subset of the attributes of all the sibling products). So each index takes more or less 30 minutes to be worked (as confirmed by checking Algolia logs, the last collection update is fired roughly 30 minutes after the original index is copied to the tmp). Two possible approaches: I found this page that talks about using a connector to push larger sets of data without worrying of payload sizes (or by using the chunkedPush you can have total control). Of course if you involve a connector you then can't work with indexName and you need a map of push task ids, but that's not a problem. Although the push is the suggested method to allow the pre-indexing transformations, the saveObjects approach seems to allow larger payloads. Assuming to map all the push task ids for collections, can I trigger the task if I have already pushed data? Whichever is the best approach, I will reimplement the job properly with a technology (most likely TypeScript, although I'm really tempted to do a Quarkus or Laravel container) that allows using their SDK, to take advantage of all the automations and optimisations of the case.

items.title: What are safe techniques to reduce latency in a backend application without compromising correctness?
items.text: I am working on a backend application and trying to reduce response latency, but I want to avoid optimizations that could introduce correctness issues. By correctness, I mean preserving accurate results, consistency, and expected behavior, while avoiding problems like stale data, race conditions, lost updates, or broken business rules. Common suggestions I see include caching, batching, indexing, query optimization, async/background jobs, and reducing network calls. However, I’m not sure which of these are generally safe first steps, and which require extra care because they can change behavior in subtle ways. So my questions are: Which latency optimizations are generally considered low-risk from a correctness perspective? Which optimizations most often introduce correctness bugs? What safeguards or validation techniques are commonly used to ensure correctness is preserved? I’m looking for practical guidance and examples from real backend systems.

items.title: How to minimize number of lookups in a Hash?
items.text: say you want to find the first duplicate in an array. you could do something like this: cnt = {}; [ 11, 22, 77, 22, 33 ].each_with_index { |x,i| j = cnt[x]; ## <1> break [x, j, i] if j cnt[x] = i ## <2> } #=> [ 22, 1, 3 ] this is O(n) as long a Ruby Hash is O(1) for lookup and insert. but still it does lookup two times ( <1> , <2> ). Is there a way to avoid it? something like this: cnt = {} some_array.each_with_index { |x,i| j = cnt.exchange( x, i ); break [x, j, i] if j } Yes, I could easily implement such a method: def exchange( k, val ) old, self[k] = self[k], val return old end but this would only hide the 2nd lookup. it would not avoid it.

The Description column contains important data. So, we cannot exclude that column from the SharePoint list API call. Could anyone suggest an efficient approach to handle a large number of rows and high volumes of column data?

items.title: Does interleaving data and instruction hurt performance in assemblies?
items.text: To expose the functions in an interpreted language as first-class C function pointers, I wrote a code to generate 'stubs' that, when executed, locates the module in which the interpreted functions reside, and calls a 'transfer' function written in C that calls the interpreter. The address of the module and the entry point of the transfer function are interleaved, as double-word data, with CPU instructions, so that their value can be discovered using PC/IP-relative addressing. And since many architectures have 'pipelining', I worry that, the way I layout the stubs may confuse the CPU microcode resulting in sub-optimal behavior, perhaps with 'instruction scheduling'? I'm really not familiar with this area. Example assembly in ARM64: static uint32_t CallStub[] = { // Relocated to the address of entry point of the transfer function. 0x00000000, 0x00000000, // Relocated to the address of code module module. 0x00000000, 0x00000000, // callxfer: // nop // nop // tumodule: // nop // nop // CallStub: // SUB SP, SP, 16 // STR X30, SP // BL +0 // MOV X2, X30 // LDR X3, tumodule // LDR X4, callxfer // BLR X4 // LDR X30, SP // ADD SP, SP, 16 // RET 0xd1000000 | (16 << 10) | (31 << 5) | 31, // SUB (immediate), 0xf9000000 | (31 << 5) | 30, // STR (immediate, unsigned offset), 0x94000000 | 1, // BL, 0xaa0003e0 | (30 << 16) | 2, // MOV (register), 0x58000000 | (0x7fffa << 5) | 3, // LDR (literal), 0x58000000 | (0x7fff7 << 5) | 4, // LDR (literal), 0xd63f0000 | (4 << 5), // BLR, 0xf8400400 | (31 << 5) | 30, // LDR (immediate), 0x91000000 | (16 << 10) | (31 << 5) | 31, // ADD (immediate), 0xd65f0000 | (30 << 5), };

items.title: Extract Matrix of Values from Matrix using Matrix of Row Numbers
items.text: I wish to efficiently extract values from a matrix ( vals ), using a single column number ( val_col ) and a matrix of row numbers ( val_rows ). Specifically, I also want my results in a matrix corresponding to val_rows . # Matrix of values. n_cols <- 3 n_rows <- 5 n_vals <- n_cols * n_rows vals <- outer( seq_len(n_rows), seq_len(n_cols), paste, sep = "x" ) vals #> [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,] "1x1" "1x2" "1x3" #> [2,] "2x1" "2x2" "2x3" #> [3,] "3x1" "3x2" "3x3" #> [4,] "4x1" "4x2" "4x3" #> [5,] "5x1" "5x2" "5x3" # Column number. val_col <- 2 # Matrix of row numbers. n_rows_out <- 4 n_vals_out <- n_cols * n_rows_out set.seed(111) val_rows <- sample.int(n_rows, size = n_vals_out, replace = TRUE) |> matrix(ncol = n_cols, byrow = TRUE) val_rows #> [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,] 3 4 3 #> [2,] 1 3 5 #> [3,] 3 4 2 #> [4,] 1 5 5 Now the result I want is a matrix like this: result <- structure( c("3x2", "1x2", "3x2", "1x2", "4x2", "3x2", "4x2", "5x2", "3x2", "5x2", "2x2", "5x2"), dim = 4:3 ) result #> [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,] "3x2" "4x2" "3x2" #> [2,] "1x2" "3x2" "5x2" #> [3,] "3x2" "4x2" "2x2" #> [4,] "1x2" "5x2" "5x2" But when I simply extract with [ my results are "flattened" into a vector. result <- vals[val_rows, val_col] result #> [1] "3x2" "1x2" "3x2" "1x2" "4x2" "3x2" "4x2" "5x2" "3x2" "5x2" "2x2" "5x2" Even when I specify drop = FALSE the matrix is not structured like val_rows . result <- vals[val_rows, val_col, drop = FALSE] result #> [,1] #> [1,] "3x2" #> [2,] "1x2" #> [3,] "3x2" #> [4,] "1x2" #> [5,] "4x2" #> [6,] "3x2" #> [7,] "4x2" #> [8,] "5x2" #> [9,] "3x2" #> [10,] "5x2" #> [11,] "2x2" #> [12,] "5x2" It seems I can simply modify the dim() in a one-liner. result <- vals[val_rows, val_col] |> `dim<-`(c(n_rows_out, n_cols)) result #> [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,] "3x2" "4x2" "3x2" #> [2,] "1x2" "3x2" "5x2" #> [3,] "3x2" "4x2" "2x2" #> [4,] "1x2" "5x2" "5x2" result <- vals[val_rows, val_col, drop = TRUE] |> `dim<-`(c(n_rows_out, n_cols)) result #> [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,] "3x2" "4x2" "3x2" #> [2,] "1x2" "3x2" "5x2" #> [3,] "3x2" "4x2" "2x2" #> [4,] "1x2" "5x2" "5x2" And obviously, I can feed the vector into matrix() to restructure it like val_rows . result <- vals[val_rows, val_col] |> matrix(ncol = n_cols, byrow = FALSE) result #> [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,] "3x2" "4x2" "3x2" #> [2,] "1x2" "3x2" "5x2" #> [3,] "3x2" "4x2" "2x2" #> [4,] "1x2" "5x2" "5x2" But if I recall, populating values via matrix() is inefficient for repetition at scale. And mustn't we pass the entire result to dim() by value, simply to modify its dim attribute in place? Anyway, these approaches redundantly "repair" the "damage" that should never have occurred in the first place. So what is the most efficient way to extract the values while retaining the structure of val_rows ?

items.title: Speeding up SPSC Queue on C++
items.text: I started learning C++ recently and I am working on SPSC Lock-Free queue. Full code of my benchmark can be found here: GitHub . What I do, is basically pinning my Producer and Consumer threads to CPU's. With that I have avg latency around 7ms with p99 13,5ms. When I set the thread priority for each thread (on 70) like this: inline int setThreadPriority() { sched_param param{}; param.sched_priority = 70; int policy = SCHED_FIFO; return pthread_setschedparam(pthread_self(), policy, &param); } My latency is getting 2x worse. With AI I came up with understanding that I am facing RT Throttling and that slows my threads. If so, what are my possibilities with improving this benchmarks? Work on isolation for my CPU ? Which is more like setting up OS itself rather than my C++ code. I am looking for directions rather than silverbullet answer, or one line of code that will improve my program. Are those set ups (on OS level) are common in low-latency environments? I am using Ubuntu 64, 2 sockets, 6 cores each socket, 4.10GHz Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-10600KF 1 NUMA node ( so no NUMA nodes ) -- EDIT -- Benchmark: Total processed:1000000 Failed write:0 Min Latency: 308ns Avg Latency: 7222098ns (7.2ms) Max Latency: 13654964ns (13.6ms) p50: 7'709'850ns (7.7ms) p90: 13'434'037ns (13ms) p99: 13'623'069ns (13.6ms) p999: 13'653'354ns (13.6ms)

items.title: React web app hitting memory limit on mobile
items.text: Our React app crashes on iOS due to memory pressure when loading workflows. The crash occurs either during the initial load or shortly after the workflow has finished loading. We have already made previous memory optimizations, but the crashes are still frequent enough to be a real user problem. The app is a web application primarily designed for desktop use. The workflow feature in particular was built with desktop memory budgets in mind, which likely contributes to the higher memory footprint on iOS. What we have tried Memoizing components and selectors with useMemo , and useCallback to avoid unnecessary re-renders and object allocation. Lazy loading the workflow itself so it is not parsed or mounted until needed. Pausing background data fetching when opening the workflow, to avoid competing memory pressure during load. Various additional optimizations across earlier iterations that reduced peak usage, but not enough to prevent crashes reliably. Environment "react": "19.1.1",

items.title: How can I make a custom programming language run closer to C-level performance?
items.text: I'm building a custom programming language and want performance close to C. Currently I'm deciding between: Generating C code and compiling with GCC/Clang Generating LLVM IR directly Using a JIT My language is statically typed and aims to feel simple like Python but run near the hardware. What usually matters most for getting near-C performance? Is LLVM generally the best choice? Is generating C first significantly worse than LLVM IR? Which compiler optimizations give the biggest gains? What common design choices make a language much slower?

items.title: Register size choice for operating on 32-bit values in 64-bit mode
items.text: 32-bit operations in 64-bit mode generally zero-extend their results to 64 bits so that subsequent instructions often give the same result regardless whether they use 32-bit registers or 64-bit registers. For example, add rax, rdx will give the same result as add eax, edx when both operands have been zero-extended from known-to-be-small values so that the sum will not exceed 32 bits. Does the choice of register size in such a situation affect code size, performance or power draw (heating), or is it a complete wash? In some of the performance-sensitive code I'm working on up to 3/4ths of the instructions could be chosen either way, and the code is intended to go flat out on all available cores. Ergo I'd rather make an informed choice. ;-)

items.title: Java Garbage Collector performance measuring advice
items.text: I am about to write my CS BSc thesis which is about: Measuring throughput, latency and STW-Pauses in JDK 21 standard JVM with G1GC and ZGC with predefined heap-sizes (2GB; 16GB) with Renaissance - by 16GB heap a default G1GC and an additional tuned G1GC will be used, as well. Time flies and a lot of paper are read. It became clear to me, that Renaissance is better for throughput (Shimchenko 2022 Analysing and predicting energy consumption of garbage collectors in openjdk), and DaCapo is more advantageous for user-experienced latency measurements (Blackburn 2025 Rethinking Java performance analysis). STW-pauses will be collected from jvm standard gc-logs with a script or smg (ideas, better ideas are welcome). I build this scenario for my examination: - Linux VM (hosted from my Windows) - not clear yet, which and why - OpenJDK 21 standard JVM - G1GC and ZGC measurements - All Renaissance BMs with default settings -> duration_ns from each benchmark, calculate and represent min, max, mean, standard deviation - JVM GC-Logs collect (min, max, mean, standard deviation) - 8 DaCapo BMs (spring, cassandra, h2, h2o, kafka, lucene, tomcat, wildfly) (min, max, mean, standard deviation) I guess this is way too much for a BSc thesis - but what are your thoughts? Of course I make clearence with my consulent, but I am curious about the opinion and suggestions of the community. I am open for any ideas, experiences with the bumpy road of the performance measurement in the JVM. It would be excellent, if someone of you could make it more focused and accurate to me. TL;DR Java Garbage Collector JVM performance measurement experience and suggestions needed for BSc thesis

items.title: Assembly x86_64 code in Windows for creating a window has jittering in the oposite edges of the left and top edges when re-scaling
items.text: I think this might be a already solved issue previously, but I still don't understand why this is happening and how to fix this in a clean and satisfactory way. I am writing a simple, basic GUI window program in x64 assembly for Windows OS because I want to have a extremely lightweight windowing setup for developing GUI applications. I know that the sane thing to do would be to just use SDL 2/3 or GLFW or even RGFW, but I really want my code to be as slim and as free from bloat as possible. In fact my plan is to try to develop some small graphical programs in x64 assembly and hand written SPIR-V for GPU interaction to see if I can get any gain (be it in performance or in memory usage) compared to another implementation of the same programs in C + Vulkan. I got to the following basic GUI program in x86_64 assembly, which I'm assembling with fasm2: format PE64 GUI 4.0 entry start ;========================================================================================= ; Constants ;========================================================================================= WS_VISIBLE = 0x10000000 WS_POPUP = 0x80000000 WS_THICKFRAME = 0x00040000 CS_VREDRAW = 0x0001 CS_HREDRAW = 0x0002 CS_OWNDC = 0x0020 IDC_ARROW = 32512 WM_DESTROY = 0x0002 WM_KEYDOWN = 0x0100 WM_NCHITTEST = 0x0084 WM_ERASEBKGND = 0x0014 WM_PAINT = 0x000F VK_ESCAPE = 0x001B HTCLIENT = 1 HTCAPTION = 2 HTLEFT = 10 HTRIGHT = 11 HTTOP = 12 HTTOPLEFT = 13 HTTOPRIGHT = 14 HTBOTTOM = 15 HTBOTTOMLEFT = 16 HTBOTTOMRIGHT = 17 ;========================================================================================= ; BSS ;========================================================================================= section '.bss' readable writeable ; MSG: HWND(8)+UINT(4)+pad(4)+WPARAM(8)+LPARAM(8)+DWORD(4)+POINT(8) = 44 bytes ? 48 msg rb 48 ; WNDCLASSA on x64: style(4)+pad(4)+lpfnWndProc(8)+cbClsExtra(4)+cbWndExtra(4)+ ; hInstance(8)+hIcon(8)+hCursor(8)+hbrBackground(8)+ ; lpszMenuName(8)+lpszClassName(8) = 72 bytes wc: .style dd ? ; UINT, offset 0 dd ? ; padding, offset 4 ? required on x64! .lpfnWndProc dq ? ; WNDPROC, offset 8 .cbClsExtra dd ? ; int, offset 16 .cbWndExtra dd ? ; int, offset 20 .hInstance dq ? ; HINSTANCE, offset 24 .hIcon dq ? ; HICON, offset 32 .hCursor dq ? ; HCURSOR, offset 40 .hbrBackground dq ? ; HBRUSH, offset 48 (0 = don't auto-erase) .lpszMenuName dq ? ; LPCSTR, offset 56 .lpszClassName dq ? ; LPCSTR, offset 64 ; RECT: four LONGs, identical in x86 and x64 rect: .left dd ? .top dd ? .right dd ? .bottom dd ? hbr dq ? ; HBRUSH handle (64-bit pointer on x64) ; PAINTSTRUCT on x64: HDC(8)+BOOL(4)+RECT(16)+BOOL(4)+BOOL(4)+BYTE[32] = 68 ? 72 bytes ; rcPaint is at offset 12 (NOT offset 8 like in x86!) ps rb 72 ;========================================================================================= ; Data ;========================================================================================= section '.data' data readable writeable class_name db 'MinimalCanvas', 0 ;========================================================================================= ; Code ;========================================================================================= section '.text' code readable executable start: ; Allocate: 32 shadow + 64 for the 8 stack args of CreateWindowExA = 96 bytes. ; PLUS 8 bytes to re-align the stack (because the OS pushed an 8-byte return address). ; Total = 104. Now RSP is perfectly 16-byte aligned before the first call! sub rsp, 104 ; Get our own hInstance (robust regardless of image base) xor ecx, ecx call [GetModuleHandleA] mov [wc.hInstance], rax ; Setup WNDCLASSA mov dword [wc.style], CS_OWNDC + CS_HREDRAW + CS_VREDRAW lea rax, [WindowProc] mov [wc.lpfnWndProc], rax ; wc.hbrBackground stays 0 (BSS is zero-initialized): tells Windows never to auto-erase ; LoadCursorA(NULL, IDC_ARROW) xor ecx, ecx mov edx, IDC_ARROW ; mov edx zero-extends to RDX on x64 ? call [LoadCursorA] mov [wc.hCursor], rax ; CreateSolidBrush(0xED9564) — Cornflower Blue background brush (0x00bbggrr) mov ecx, 0xED9564 call [CreateSolidBrush] mov [hbr], rax lea rax, [class_name] mov [wc.lpszClassName], rax ; RegisterClassA(&wc) lea rcx, [wc] call [RegisterClassA] ; CreateWindowExA(0, class_name, NULL, WS_POPUP|WS_VISIBLE, ; 100, 100, 800, 600, NULL, NULL, hInstance, NULL) ; Args 1-4 go in registers; args 5-12 go on stack at [rsp+32]..[rsp+88] xor ecx, ecx ; dwExStyle = 0 lea rdx, [class_name] ; lpClassName xor r8, r8 ; lpWindowName = NULL mov r9d, WS_POPUP + WS_VISIBLE ; dwStyle (Removed WS_THICKFRAME to drop DWM top line) mov qword [rsp+32], 100 ; X mov qword [rsp+40], 100 ; Y mov qword [rsp+48], 800 ; nWidth mov qword [rsp+56], 600 ; nHeight mov qword [rsp+64], 0 ; hWndParent = NULL mov qword [rsp+72], 0 ; hMenu = NULL mov rax, [wc.hInstance] mov [rsp+80], rax ; hInstance mov qword [rsp+88], 0 ; lpParam = NULL call [CreateWindowExA] msg_loop: ; GetMessageA(&msg, NULL, 0, 0) lea rcx, [msg] xor edx, edx xor r8d, r8d xor r9d, r9d call [GetMessageA] test eax,

items.title: How to fix lag in video editing software while exporting videos?
items.text: I am a beginner in video editing and facing performance issues while exporting videos. The software becomes very slow and sometimes freezes during rendering. I am using basic editing tools with simple cuts and transitions, but still facing lag issues. My system has limited RAM and I am not sure if the problem is due to hardware or software settings. Can anyone suggest how to optimize video editing performance and reduce lag during export? Any beginner-friendly tips would be helpful.

items.title: Micro benchmarking: how to measure multi-threaded CPU time?
items.text: I'm attempting to benchmark a custom queue implementation with X consumer and Y producer threads. A single run takes on average ~68ms, and I've noticed that my benchmarking library sometimes produces false negative performance regression warnings on code that hasn't been changed. I believe that this is likely due to adverse thread scheduling, or due to thread creation taking slightly longer than normal. I've decided that measuring CPU time would be a better way to benchmark this code, the problem is that I'm not sure how to measure it. To my understanding using CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID would return the sum total of all thread CPU time usage, which is fine when comparing run-to-run, but it can be a little weird to look at as a human. I'm also not sure how I would go about getting related numbers as throughput in items/s without just using wall clock time again. EDIT: since it was asked I'm running Fedora Linux, x86-64. The project is written in Rust and uses the Criterion crate/benchmarking framework.

items.title: Why is DELETE much slower than TRUNCATE even when deleting all rows from a table in PostgreSQL?
items.text: In an application cleanup job, I need to remove all rows from a table regularly. Table: CREATE TABLE session_logs ( id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, session_id TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, payload JSONB ); When I run: DELETE FROM session_logs; It takes a long time and generates a lot of disk activity. But: TRUNCATE session_logs; Is almost instant. What I expected : Both operations remove all rows, so I expected similar performance. What actually happens : DELETE is very slow, TRUNCATE is almost instant What I tried : Removing indexes Running VACUUM after DELETE My question is : From PostgreSQL’s internal behavior, why is DELETE so much slower than TRUNCATE when removing all rows from a table?

items.title: Improve the performance of code that builds an object by comparing two objects
items.text: I need a faster way to build one object from another in PowerShell. I have a PowerShell array of Strings with 10,000 hostnames: $hostnames = @("server1","server2","server3"..."server10000") And a PowerShell array of custom objects with NoteProperty "hostname" and "IP". Both Note Properties are Strings. The array has 50,000 elements. $inventory[0].name="server1" $inventory[0].ip="10.0.0.1" $inventory[1].name="server2" $inventory[1].ip="10.0.0.2" I need to build a new object of the elements in $inventory but only for the matching elements in $hostnames. I wrote this code: $newList = @() foreach($name in $hostnames){ $newList += $inventory | Where-Object {$_.name -eq $name} } This code works...eventually. With 10k elements in $hostnames, it has to cycle through the 50k elements of $inventory ten thousand times. This takes a very long time. How can I optimize this code? Is there a .Net class I should be using instead of the PowerShell Array? Is there an algorithm I should use beside the for-each loop with Where-Object?

items.title: Best approach to implement cascading filters (45 columns, up to 10k values each) in React + Spring Boot with SQL backend
items.text: I have a React UI with a Spring Boot backend that retrieves data from a SQL database . The screen contains around 45 filter columns , where filters work in a cascading way. For example: Selecting a value in Filter 1 determines the available values in Filter 2 Selecting Filter 2 further restricts values for Filter 3 This continues for up to 45 filters Each filter can have up to ~10,000 possible values in the database. Currently, the backend runs SQL queries with WHERE conditions based on selected filters to return the next set of possible values. However, I am concerned about performance and scalability , since: The number of filters is large (45) Each filter can contain many values Queries may become complex as more filters are applied The UI may trigger frequent requests as users change filters Example simplified flow: User selects value for column1 Backend runs a query to fetch valid values for column2 User selects value for column2 Backend fetches values for column3 And so on... Example query pattern: SELECT DISTINCT column2 FROM data_table WHERE column1 = ? Then: SELECT DISTINCT column3 FROM data_table WHERE column1 = ? AND column2 = ? My questions: What is the recommended backend design pattern for implementing large cascading filters like this? Should this continue to be handled via SQL queries , or is it better to use something like a search/indexing engine (e.g., Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) ? Are there common optimization techniques (caching, precomputed aggregates, materialized views, etc.) that work well for this type of filtering system? Any architectural or performance recommendations would be appreciated.

items.title: How to efficiently fetch data from Metropolitan Museum of Art Collection API?
items.text: I am working on a project where I need to fetch and display a large dataset (similar to the Metropolitan Museum of Art collection API). The dataset contains thousands of records, including images and metadata. This project also includes a digital membership card feature for users, where members can access personalized content and saved artworks. Because of this, performance and scalability are very important. Currently, I am fetching all data at once, which is causing slow response times and performance issues on the frontend. Here is my current code: const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.get('/artworks', async (req, res) => { try { const response = await fetch('https://api.example.com/artworks'); const data = await response.json(); res.json(data); } catch (error) { res.status(500).send(error.message); } }); app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server running on port 3000')); 🔴 Problem: API response is very large Page load is slow Not scalable for thousands of records User-specific data (for digital membership card) may increase load further ❓ What I want to achieve: Implement pagination (limit & offset or cursor-based) Improve performance and reduce load time Efficiently handle user-specific data for membership features My Questions: What is the best way to implement pagination in this case? Should I handle pagination on the API side or frontend? Are there better approaches like lazy loading, caching, or database indexing? How can I efficiently scale this with user features like digital membership card access? Any suggestions or best practices would be really helpful. Thanks!

items.title: Audit table: efficient query for recent rows when filtering on non-partition column of TimescaleDB hypertable
items.text: I have an audit table using a TimescaleDB hypertable: CREATE TABLE log.data ( modified timestamptz not null default clock_timestamp(), table_schema text not null, table_name text not null, id int, dt timestamptz not null, operation text not null, cols text[], row_data jsonb ); CREATE INDEX ON log.data (modified); CREATE INDEX ON log.data (dt, id); SELECT create_hypertable('log.data', 'dt'); ALTER TABLE log.data SET (timescaledb.compress, timescaledb.compress_orderby = 'dt DESC, modified DESC', timescaledb.compress_segmentby = 'id'); I use triggers to log changes ( INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE ) from two source tables. We need to update historical data very frequent. Problem We have two typical query patterns, and both should be reasonably fast: ... WHERE id = ? AND dt = ? ... WHERE id = ? and dt >= ? and dt <= ? ... WHERE modified >= ? and modified <= ? ... WHERE modified >= now() - interval '10 minutes' The first two queries are quite fast, even with a large amount of data. The last two take several seconds. The execution plan shows that: many chunks are scanned chunk exclusion does not work query performance will degrade as the table grows Approaches that did not help: I cannot partition by modified, because bulk updates would likely produce very uneven chunk sizes. Chunk skipping also does not help, because updates to historical data distribute recent modified values across many chunks. The same problem appears if the hypertable is partitioned by modified and chunk skipping is used on dt. Adding a second dimension with add_dimension() did not improve query performance either; it only resulted in a very large number of chunks. Ideas: change chunk size dynamically like automated tuning some kind of "hot" table with only recent changes What is the recommended design for efficiently querying recent changes (modified) in a TimescaleDB hypertable that is primarily partitioned by a different time column (dt)? Is there a best practice for this use case (e.g. two hypertables, "hot/cold" tables, different indexing strategies, or schema redesign)? Or is TimescaleDB fundamentally not a good fit for this kind of audit/logging workload with two independent time dimensions?

items.title: Why does this distributed matrix multiplication become slower when using two nodes?
items.text:
The following ScaLAPACK code gets 3.8 Tflops on one node, but only 1.5 Tflops on two nodes. After that, it scales well. I don't understand why we get an initial slowdown. The matrix is about 50K x 50K. The interconnect between nodes is 12.5 GB/s, so communication should not take more than a few seconds on a total time of several minutes. I tried block sizes 2000, 128, 4000, but this gives the same behaviour. I run the code with OMP_PLACES=cores srun --cpu-bind=cores using 1 task per node, and 128 cores per task (number of cores per node). #include <mpi.h> #include <stdio.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include <math.h> #define MPI_SAFE(fncall) \ do { \ int retcode = (fncall); \ if (retcode != MPI_SUCCESS) { \ char msg[1024]; \ int resultlen; \ MPI_Error_string(retcode, msg, &resultlen); \ fprintf(stderr, "Call %s failed with %s(%d)
", \ #fncall, msg, retcode); \ MPI_Abort(MPI_COMM_WORLD, retcode); \ } \ } while (0) extern void Cblacs_get(int, int, int*); extern void Cblacs_gridinit(int*, const char*, int, int); extern void Cblacs_gridinfo(int, int*, int*, int*, int*); extern void Cblacs_gridexit(int); extern int numroc_(int*, int*, int*, int*, int*); extern void descinit_(int*, int*, int*, int*, int*, int*, int*, int*, int*, int*); extern void pdgemm_(char*, char*, int*, int*, int*, double*, double*, int*, int*, int*, double*, int*, int*, int*, double*, double*, int*, int*, int*); int main(int argc, char **argv) { MPI_SAFE(MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)); int s, p; MPI_SAFE(MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &s)); MPI_SAFE(MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &p)); if (argc != 4) { printf("Usage: %s M K N
", argv[0]); MPI_SAFE(MPI_Abort(MPI_COMM_WORLD, EXIT_FAILURE)); } int M = atoi(argv[1]); int K = atoi(argv[2]); int N = atoi(argv[3]); /* As per netlib documentation, performance is not that sensitive as * long as extreme cases are avoided. Can confirm experimentally. */ int block = 256; int p0 = (int)sqrt(p); while (p % p0) p0--; int p1 = p / p0; int ictxt; Cblacs_get(0,0,&ictxt); Cblacs_gridinit(&ictxt,"Row",p0,p1); int myrow, mycol; Cblacs_gridinfo(ictxt, &p0, &p1, &myrow, &mycol); int zero = 0; /* Local */ int m_A = numroc_(&M, &block, &myrow, &zero, &p0); int k_A = numroc_(&K, &block, &mycol, &zero, &p1); int k_B = numroc_(&K, &block, &myrow, &zero, &p0); int n_B = numroc_(&N, &block, &mycol, &zero, &p1); int m_C = m_A; int n_C = n_B; double *A = malloc(m_A * k_A * sizeof(double)); double *B = malloc(k_B * n_B * sizeof(double)); double *C = malloc(m_C * n_C * sizeof(double)); #pragma omp parallel for for(int j = 0;j < k_A; j++) { for(int i = 0; i < m_A; i++) { A[i + j * m_A] = 1.0; } } #pragma omp parallel for for (int j = 0;j < n_B; j++) { for (int i = 0; i < k_B; i++) { B[i + j * k_B] = 1.0; } } #pragma omp parallel for for (int j = 0; j < n_C; j++) { for (int i = 0; i < m_C; i++) { C[i + j * m_C] = 0.0; } } int descA[9], descB[9], descC[9], info; descinit_(descA, &M, &K, &block, &block, &zero, &zero, &ictxt, &m_A, &info); descinit_(descB, &K, &N, &block, &block, &zero, &zero, &ictxt, &k_B, &info); descinit_(descC, &M, &N, &block, &block, &zero, &zero, &ictxt, &m_C, &info); double alpha = 1.0, beta = 0.0; int ia = 1, ja = 1; MPI_SAFE(MPI_Barrier(MPI_COMM_WORLD)); double start = MPI_Wtime(); pdgemm_("N", "N", &M, &N, &K, &alpha, A, &ia, &ja, descA, B, &ia, &ja, descB, &beta, C, &ia, &ja, descC); MPI_SAFE(MPI_Barrier(MPI_COMM_WORLD)); double stop = MPI_Wtime(); if (s == 0){ printf("%lf
", 2.0 * 1e-9 * M * N * K / (stop - start)); } free(A); free(B); free(C); Cblacs_gridexit(ictxt); MPI_SAFE(MPI_Finalize()); return 0; }

items.title: Does flipping from/to dates within date index make it more selective for SQL Server
items.text: In one of my projects I have an entity with startDate (from) and expiryDate (to): @Entity public class BlacklistedItem { //... @Column(nullable = false) private LocalDateTime startDate; @Column(nullable = false) private LocalDateTime expiryDate; } Here's the JPA query we use to load the items: @Query(""" select b from BlacklistedItem b where current_timestamp between b.startDate and b.expiryDate """) List<BlacklistedActiveIsin> findAllMatchingDate(); For the faster search I've created this index: changes: - createIndex: clustered: false columns: - column: name: start_date - column: name: expiry_date indexName: ix__blacklisted_items_start_date_expiry_date tableName: blacklisted_items unique: false At review time I was advised to flip start_date and expiry_date which would make the index more selective as current_timestamp is always shifting right making condition current_timestamp >= start_date more likely to match comparing to current_timestamp <= expiry_date . Here's the excution plan for the query without index: |--Clustered Index Scan(OBJECT:([blacklisted_items].[pk_blacklisted_items] AS [i]), WHERE:(getdate()>=[blacklisted_items].[start_date] as [i].[start_date] AND getdate()<=[blacklisted_items].[expiry_date] as [i].[expiry_date])) This plan is for original index ( start_date goes first): |--Clustered Index Scan(OBJECT:([blacklisted_items].[pk_blacklisted_items] AS [i]), WHERE:(getdate()>=[blacklisted_items].[start_date] as [i].[start_date] AND getdate()<=[blacklisted_items].[expiry_date] as [i].[expiry_date])) And this one is for modified index ( expiry_date goes first): |--Clustered Index Scan(OBJECT:([blacklisted_items].[pk_blacklisted_items] AS [i]), WHERE:(getdate()>=[blacklisted_items].[start_date] as [i].[start_date] AND getdate()<=[blacklisted_items].[expiry_date] as [i].[expiry_date])) All the three plans are effectively the same, so I've got two questions: does flipping dates really makes the index more selective? for the sake of better performance should I replace my original query with the one relying on fast return for expiry_date ? select * from blacklisted_items i where current_timestamp <= i.expiry_date and current_timestamp >= i.start_date; The idea for the query change is based on the assumption about lower comparation count for the data like start_date expiry_date 01.01.2026 01.01.2099 02.01.2026 01.02.2026 02.01.2026 01.03.2026 If we first check start_date (this is what between does) the query does 6 comparations (2 per each row), and if expiry_date is checked first the query does only 4 comparations, i.e. we save 1 comparation per any item having expiry_date in the past.

items.title: Optimization of entropy-based wordle solver on haskell
items.text: My wordle solver seems to be slow, i would like to ask your opinions on how i can speed it up further I did some optimizations i could think of, but speed still seems to be bad As far as i understand, bottleneck is in filterByResult function Specifically in eqZip and zippedEq "sub"-functions I don't see myself any way to optimize filterByResult , maybe to think about better way of doing passesCounts at most But passesChars is first arg to && , and program works faster this way; so i don't think it makes sense to work with counts for now Types: import qualified Data.ByteString as BS import qualified Data.Map.Strict as Map -- Allowed guesses, allowed answers type GuessCtx = ([WordleWord], [WordleWord]) data GuessResult = GRWrong | GROtherPlace | GRCorrect type WordleWord = BS.ByteString Code for filterByResult filterByResult :: WordleWord -> [GuessResult] -> (WordleWord -> Bool) filterByResult guess res w = passesChars && passesCounts where zippedGuess :: [(GuessResult, Word8)] zippedGuess = zip res (BS.unpack guess) eqZip :: [Bool] eqZip = BS.zipWith (==) guess w countChars :: Map.Map Word8 Int -> (GuessResult, Word8) -> Map.Map Word8 Int countChars m (GRWrong, gc) = Map.insertWith (+) gc 0 m countChars m (_, gc) = Map.insertWith (+) gc 1 m countedChars :: Map.Map Word8 Int countedChars = foldl' countChars Map.empty zippedGuess passesCounts :: Bool passesCounts = Map.foldlWithKey' filterWithMap True countedChars where filterWithMap :: Bool -> Word8 -> Int -> Bool filterWithMap False _ _ = False filterWithMap True c n | n == 0 = c `BS.notElem` w | otherwise = BS.count c w >= n passesChars :: Bool passesChars = foldl' filterWithChars True zippedEq where zippedEq :: [(GuessResult, Bool)] zippedEq = zip res eqZip filterWithChars :: Bool -> (GuessResult, Bool) -> Bool filterWithChars False _ = False filterWithChars True (GRCorrect, isEq) = isEq filterWithChars True (_, isEq) = not isEq Another area of interest is entropy calculation: wordEntropy :: WordleWord -> [WordleWord] -> Double wordEntropy w gs = sum $ do res <- possibleResults let newGuessList = filter (filterByResult w res) gs let ngCount :: Double = fromIntegral $ length newGuessList guard $ ngCount > 0 let gCount :: Double = fromIntegral $ length gs let probability = ngCount / gCount let entropy = -logBase 2 probability pure $ probability * entropy avg :: (Fractional r) => [r] -> r avg vals = sum vals / fromIntegral (length vals) You can see i am using sum instead of avg here I did it because avg makes this code a lot slower(10x or more) And for my current tests sum works ok in terms of solving wordle(at least with first 100 answers from dictionary) Actually i am not sure why avg occurs such a performance penalty I thought it could be because of traversing list twice(sum and length), but by rewriting avg using foldl' to do it in one pass i won nothing in terms of performance(and, probably, GHC is smart enough to optimize it) Maybe it can make sense to add some check to filter possibleResults before calling filterByResult (to filter impossible results) But i am afraid it'll be as costly to do, as filterByResult , so for now i just use guard after filterByResult selectBestNextWord :: GuessCtx -> WordleWord selectBestNextWord (gs, _) = snd $ foldl' entIter (0, "") gs where entIter :: (Double, WordleWord) -> WordleWord -> (Double, WordleWord) entIter prev@(maxEnt, _) curW = let wEnt = wordEntropy curW gs in if wEnt > maxEnt then (wEnt, curW) else prev Main just reads dict from file, calculates first word to be used and then loops through all answers from dict and attempts to solve each in at most 6 guesses I would like to improve speed, to be able to see results in 20 minutes at least, and to have at least something like "interactive" speed for solving 1 word(see iterateWord function) when using dict of ~2k words Maybe it is possible to achieve greater speed by using already precalculated 2-3 words, so we'll calculate entropy for small range of words after that - but i would like to avoid using "external" words that my program couldn't calculate itself On occasion of asking experienced people here - i would be very grateful if you have some advices/remarks about code style or really anything about this code Times now(with profiling, measured with +RTS -sstderr -p ): 100 words - 1.9s 200 words - 24.5s 500 words - 102.1s Times without prifiling(measured with time ): 100 words - 0.8s 200 words - 8.2s 500 words - 33.7s Also i did try to use parallel module inside wordEntropy to parallelize list evaluation, but all my attempts resulted in the same or worse performance(and using top i saw once core being 100% loaded, while second was 1-5% only) PS: i did use -threaded GHC flag and did pass -N1 / -N2 flags to program when running, maybe it's just my old CPU's behavior though Code for it was like this: wordEntropy w gs = sum $ withStrategy (parList rpar) $ do and wordEntropy w gs = sum $ withStrategy (parList rseq) $ do I am not experienced with parallel , so mayb

items.title: How to optimize real-time image restoration performance in a Flask-based Deepfake defense system?
items.text: I am developing a system that integrates "Source Verifiable" and "Content Decipherable" into a single service workflow. The system uses Robust Watermarking to ensure source authenticity and Tamper Detection (IID-Net) to pinpoint the exact location and scope of any modifications, providing clear visualization evidence. 1) Design Principles and Decision-Making: The system uses a dual-track approach: Global Traceability (END): Capable of reading and verifying source ownership even after common transmissions like re-compression, scaling, cropping, or minor geometric distortions. Regional Localization (IID-Net): Outputs pixel-level masks and difference summaries for local manipulations such as splicing, smudging, or replacement. 2) Risk Model and Protection Strategy: In real-world scenarios like "Image Laundering," single-path protection often fails. By combining Robust Traceability with Regional Localization, our system fills the gap where local changes are made without altering the global structure. This is particularly relevant for news fact-checking, platform governance, and judicial evidence. Question: What are the best practices for implementing such a dual-track inference pipeline in a Flask environment to minimize latency for high-resolution images?
