items.title: Quick answers to your questions, more detail follows... How large is your automation suite? 7 suites…
items.text: Quick answers to your questions, more detail follows... How large is your automation suite? 7 suites. Biggest is 450 tests, average ~150. What tends to break most often? It depends. Most daily failures were due to environment and test data, not site updates. For site updates, minor changes were usually locator updates with some minor coding while major changes were a little of everything... locators, code, page objects, etc. How much time goes into maintaining tests? It depends. For me, the average day was ~30 mins to investigate all failures in all suites. For teammates, it took 1-2 hours. If you are talking about maintenance from site updates, it varied depending on the size of the updates... maybe a day or less for small changes to a couple weeks for large changes. The details... I recently retired but I did automation using Selenium for almost 12 years. Right before I retired, I started converting one of our new Selenium suites to Playwright. With Playwright the tooling was awesome and the execution speed was almost twice as fast as Selenium. There were a lot of things about Playwright that was better than Selenium but I wasn't around long enough after converting the first suite to see the maintenance difference from Selenium suites, if there was one. I worked at a credit union (like a bank but different). We had 7 C#/Selenium-based test suites hooked into Azure DevOps. The biggest suite was ~450 tests while most suites were ~100-150 tests. We ran all suites every night (~1300 tests) and investigated all failures the next morning. The average day for me to investigate all failures was probably around 30 mins. That said, I wrote most of the tests and had been working on the suites for years so I was faster than most. I think others could do the same thing in an hour or two. At this point, the suites were very mature. Failures were rarely due to our code/tests unless it was an expected change that broke tests. Most failures were intermittent failures... most times it was something was down/wrong with the environment at the time the test ran, etc. but they ran successfully the next morning. Sometimes it was something wrong with the data... the account we were using was in a bad state or out of money, etc. but could be fixed easily... not a code/test problem. In some cases, the UI was updated/changed... sometimes it was minor which needed locator changes, other times it was a complete flow change which required a lot of coding/refactoring but those were typically changes due to projects that we were aware of and expected. The amount of time needed to fix them really depended on the scope of the changes. The biggest changes I can remember took probably a couple weeks to fix and were major flow changes to applying for a loan, etc. which required a complete flow change, updates/changes to multiple page objects, some new page objects, etc. All suites use the page object model. You really can't scale projects efficiently without them. It makes maintenance about as easy as it can be. Other rules we used were locator types/preferences... 1. IDs, 2. CSS selectors, 3. XPath only when required (locating an element by contained text or DOM traversal... always use relative XPaths). Crafting a good locator is part science, part art and takes time to learn. I think writing good locators is one big key to lowering maintenance needs/costs. Hopefully that answers your questions. Feel free to ask more questions...

items.title: For posterity, I added this to the file to get it to compile as a single, standalone executable: {-#…
items.text: For posterity, I added this to the file to get it to compile as a single, standalone executable: {-# LANGUAGE OverloadedStrings #-} {-# LANGUAGE ScopedTypeVariables #-} import Control.Monad import Data.List import Data.Word possibleResults :: [[GuessResult]] possibleResults = replicateM 6 [GRWrong, GROtherPlace, GRCorrect] main :: IO () main = do gs <- BS.split 10 <$> BS.getContents print (selectBestNextWord (gs, [])) My experiments will be done with ghc-9.8.1. To get a wordlist, I filtered /usr/share/dict/words to the six-letter words, chunked the result into 500 equally sized contiguous chunks, and took the first word from each chunk. I will use ghc -O2 test to compile throughout (and ./test <test.txt to execute). Initial run with the code from the question is 16.1s. I think this is probably partly an exercise to learn Haskell, so before optimizing, I'm going to suggest some cleanups that use library functions instead of re-implementing them by hand, so you can see what idiomatic Haskell looks like. First, we can define countedChars using fromListWith . -- was data GuessResult = GRWrong | GROtherPlace | GRCorrect zippedGuess :: [(GuessResult, Word8)] zippedGuess = zip res (BS.unpack guess) countChars :: Map.Map Word8 Int -> (GuessResult, Word8) -> Map.Map Word8 Int countChars m (GRWrong, gc) = Map.insertWith (+) gc 0 m countChars m (_, gc) = Map.insertWith (+) gc 1 m countedChars :: Map.Map Word8 Int countedChars = foldl' countChars Map.empty zippedGuess -- suggestion data GuessResult = GRWrong | GROtherPlace | GRCorrect deriving (Eq, Ord, Read, Show) countedChars :: Map.Map Word8 Int countedChars = Map.fromListWith (+) $ zipWith (\gc singleRes -> (gc, if singleRes == GRWrong then 0 else 1)) (BS.unpack guess) res We can define passesChars using and and zipWith . -- was eqZip :: [Bool] eqZip = BS.zipWith (==) guess w passesChars :: Bool passesChars = foldl' filterWithChars True zippedEq where zippedEq :: [(GuessResult, Bool)] zippedEq = zip res eqZip filterWithChars :: Bool -> (GuessResult, Bool) -> Bool filterWithChars False _ = False filterWithChars True (GRCorrect, isEq) = isEq filterWithChars True (_, isEq) = not isEq -- suggestion passesChars :: Bool passesChars = and $ zipWith (\singleRes singleEq -> (singleRes == GRCorrect) == singleEq) res (BS.zipWith (==) guess w) We can define passesCounts using and , too; mapWithKey is kinda like zipWith ! -- was passesCounts :: Bool passesCounts = Map.foldlWithKey' filterWithMap True countedChars where filterWithMap :: Bool -> Word8 -> Int -> Bool filterWithMap False _ _ = False filterWithMap True c n | n == 0 = c `BS.notElem` w | otherwise = BS.count c w >= n -- suggestion passesCounts :: Bool passesCounts = and $ Map.mapWithKey (\c n -> if n == 0 then c `BS.notElem` w else BS.count c w >= n) countedChars Your selectBestNextWord is a secret maximumOn . ...well, it would be, except that the standard library doesn't include maximumOn for lists. I've thought this was an oversight ever since the introduction of sortOn . But in such situations, it's often better to define the generic thing, whose behavior can be guessed from the name and standard library's conventions, and then specialize it, rather than writing a completely special thing that must be read all at once. Like this: -- was selectBestNextWord :: GuessCtx -> WordleWord selectBestNextWord (gs, _) = snd $ foldl' entIter (0, "") gs where entIter :: (Double, WordleWord) -> WordleWord -> (Double, WordleWord) entIter prev@(maxEnt, _) curW = let wEnt = wordEntropy curW gs in if wEnt > maxEnt then (wEnt, curW) else prev -- suggestion import Data.Ord maximumOn :: Ord b => (a -> b) -> [a] -> a maximumOn f = fst . maximumBy (comparing snd) . map (\a -> (a, f a)) selectBestNextWord :: GuessCtx -> WordleWord selectBestNextWord (gs, _) = maximumOn (flip wordEntropy gs) gs With these changes, the program is a little bit faster, 9.25s, without substantially changing the algorithm. I'd like to stress, though, that these changes were made for readability; the fact that it improved performance too is nice, but was not the goal. Now on to optimization. The first thing that jumps out to me is that you are iterating over the entire word list once for each possible result, which is 3^6 = 729 times. It's probably going to be better to iterate once, computing the correct result, and partition the list using it. Yes, this means we will be throwing away almost all of the changes from above... such is life in iterative improvement land! First the function that computes a result: computeResult :: WordleWord -> WordleWord -> [GuessResult] computeResult guess w = go mismatches0 (BS.unpack guess) (BS.unpack w) where mismatches0 :: Map.Map Word8 Int mismatches0 = Map.fromListWith (+) $ BS.zipWith (\singleGuess singleW -> (singleW, if singleGuess == singleW then 0 else 1)) guess w go :: Map.Map Word8 Int -> [Word8] -> [Word8] -> [GuessResult] go mismatches (g:gs) (w:ws) | g == w = GRCorrect : go mismatches gs ws | otherwise = case Map.f

items.title: (Edited: I had my answer backwards. Also added demo fiddle that compares index reads.) I've always c…
items.text: (Edited: I had my answer backwards. Also added demo fiddle that compares index reads.) I've always considered the inability to directly support ranges as a shortcoming of SQL Server (and likely most or all other RDMS's). As far as I know, there is no efficient way to index ranges, which would be comparable to a one-dimensional spatial index. So, the best we can do is define separate columns for the range-start and range-end values, and create an index that includes both. For a query that filters the rows with an expression like WHERE t.StartValue <= @SearchValue AND @SearchValue < t.EndValue , the execution plan may perform a range scan for the first indexed column between @SearchValue and one end of the index or the other, and then test the other half of the condition on a row-by-row basis on the second indexed column. This would be almost like a half-of-the-index scan or part-index scan (my made-up terms). So which is better - an index on (StartValue, EndValue) or an index on (EndValue, StartValue) ? With no other assumptions, it is a tie. However, if you are dealing with dates and assume that more queries will be looking for more recent values than for older values, an index on (EndValue, StartValue) should be marginally more efficient, because a partial index scan for @SearchValue < t.EndValue would include fewer index rows than a t.StartValue <= @SearchValue scan. On the other hand, if searching for older dates, the reverse is true. See this db<>fiddle . Given a set of overlapping 1, 2, and 3 day ranges for the month of January: A search for ranges that include an earlier date 2026-01-05 has fewer index reads when using the (StartDate, EndDate) index. A search for ranges that include a a later date 2026-01-25 has fewer index reads when using the (EndDate, StartDate) index. Bottom line, the best answer depends on how your typical search dates compare with the ranges to be searched.

items.title: An index seek will only be able to do a range seek on one of the columns. it can either seek on expi…
items.text: An index seek will only be able to do a range seek on one of the columns. it can either seek on expiry_date >= current_timestamp or it can seek on start_date <= current_timestamp making assumptions about your data probably most of the rows are not future dated so start_date <= current_timestamp won't eliminate many rows? If you have a large proportion of expired rows then yes putting that as the leading column would allow that seek to be used to eliminate more rows. If you have a large number of non expired rows this might not be very useful too though. The index would need to be "covering" to get an index seek in that case. Your execution plans are showing clustered index scans for all options so aren't using the index.

items.title: One thing developers must keep in mind is that SQL is not a procedural language. It's a calculus: Th…
items.text: One thing developers must keep in mind is that SQL is not a procedural language. It's a calculus: The engine will compile your query and, based on heuristics, try to plan the most efficient way to solve it. That said, you don't know what the engine will do first. SELECT FROM A JOIN B ON A.X = B.Y WHERE A.ID = 11 AND B.ID = 22 SELECT FROM B JOIN A ON B.Y = A.X WHERe B.ID = 22 AND A.ID = 11 That's a good example of this idea. The engine will first filter the smaller/better index/table. My advice is not to create a single index the way you did. Instead, create one index for each date column. Better two index seeks in the hand than one flying around.

items.title: This is expected behavior due the how GDI was grandfathered in to the world of the Desktop Window Ma…
items.text: This is expected behavior due the how GDI was grandfathered in to the world of the Desktop Window Manager (DWM). If your ultimate goal is to paint the window with an API like OpenGL or DirectWhatever, you can ignore this and move on. Ignoring details (most of which I don't know), the key to DWM is that composites the screen image by treating each window as a quad and a texture that contains the pixels for that window. The Jank When you drag the left side of a window to the left, the quad is updated immediately. So, on the next frame, the window occupies a wider area. But there is a delay between when the left side moves and when the texture gets updated. The old texture is still anchored to the left side, but it doesn't have pixel data for the newly exposed strip on the right. So it looks as though the window moved to the left. A few frames later, the texture is updated, and it appears the right side snaps back to its original position. You have the same issue when drag-sizing the right border to the right, it's just harder to see because the anchor point of the texture doesn't change. The jank will be in how smoothly the edge you're dragging keeps up with the mouse cursor. Causes of the Delay If the window has GPU-native rendering, this takes rarely more than one frame. But GDI is rendered in software by the CPU, possibly directly into video memory but perhaps into system memory. Unlike a video game which constantly generates new frames, rendering (eventually) begins in reaction to the device context invalidation(s) caused by the resize event(s). I say "eventually" because the WM_PAINT won't be delivered until the message queue is otherwise empty. Because the client area is now larger, GDI first has to dump the current offscreen bitmap and create a new larger one. Then it handles the painting commands to update it (often with a little bit of batching). Meanwhile, the DWM is effectively polling to see when the GDI rendering appears to be done, at which point it blits GDI's offscreen bitmap to its texture (which it probably also had to resize). Reducing the Delay Can you make a GDI-painted window not quite as janky? Maybe. Based on nothing but a gut-feel, I wonder whether CS_CLASSDC hampers the synchronization between the GDI and the DWM. Given how uncommon CS_CLASSDC is, I wouldn't expect it to be as optimized. CS_HREDRAW and CS_VREDRAW cause the entire client area to be invalidated when the window is resized. That means you have the repaint all the pixels. For a window with dynamic layout, that's usually necessary. For your blue window, that's overkill. That said, the difference in time between filling the entire client area versus just the newly-exposed area probably isn't the bottleneck when you're just filling with a solid color. Avoid WM_ERASEBKGND and make sure your WM_PAINT handler covers everything in the update region. You're already doing that. Double-buffer the GDI rendering. This is counterintuitive and contrary to the DWM best practices , but I find this generally reduces the redraw delay. I suspect the single bitblit to the device context makes it clear to the DWM that it's time to copy the pixel data. Consider using a window frame if it makes sense (e.g., WS_THICKFRAME | WS_CAPTION). The system's non-client frame code seems to be optimized to work well with the DWM. It might take a moment for the pixels within the frame to update after a resize, but the frame will be where it should be, making the delay less visually jarring. Make your rendering DPI aware and mark the program as DPI aware, especially if you use GDI text.

items.title: You can use Hash#update or its alias merge! to insert x => i . If the key happens to exist, the meth…
items.text: You can use Hash#update or its alias merge! to insert x => i . If the key happens to exist, the method's block will be called. The block arguments will match your [x, j, i] array, i.e. key, old value, and new value. If you move everything into a method, you can simply return the block arguments from within the block.

items.title: It would probably help to narrow your design space a bit. AI-powered or not, we first have to confro…
items.text: It would probably help to narrow your design space a bit. AI-powered or not, we first have to confront the fact that "engaging", "attractive", "long", and "short" are all subjective terms that highly depend on the video, and also highly depend on what you intend to do with the short videos thus produced. First, can you post a few links of some original "long videos" that you plan to shorten using a tool like this? 3-5 will do, but they need to be specific, concrete videos that actually exist and are real and are something that a human or AI tool could analyze. If you cannot post a link to a specific video in this forum, explain why. Also add a sentence or two about why that video in particular is of interest to you. Then, for each of those 3-5 videos you linked, have a concrete description of what exactly a "short" version of that video would look like. If such a short video actually exists, link it. If not, write a play-by-play script of the video. Finally, explain what you want to do with that video thus produced, and what you want out of it. Doing this should give you a much more concrete idea of what sorts of tools you would need to accomplish your task.

items.title: This sounds like an AI vibe-coding prompt, or a brief that you give to a developer you're hiring for…
items.text: This sounds like an AI vibe-coding prompt, or a brief that you give to a developer you're hiring for a reasonably large piece of work. It's far too broad for a Stackoverflow post, certainly - even in this open-ended category we need something reasonably focused in order to be able to give an answer in a reasonable timescale - we're volunteers, after all, not a free consultancy. Also it doesn't even make sense - either you want to a step-by-step guide covering how to do absolutely everything so that it all works (your first point), or you want some pointers to which tools and design ideas you might want to consider in order that you can go and build it yourself. Which is it? You don't seem to know what you're asking. Lastly, and I mean this seriously - if you genuinely intend to code this yourself but currently don't even know how to code in JavaScript, or in any other language, then you are biting off way more than you can chew at this stage. It's nice to have an ambition like this, but you need to take a step back and go away and spend a long chunk of time learning and practicsing the language via a book or course, and writing simple programs to understand the key concepts before you even think of attempting something like this. Becoming a competent programmer who can tackle a project like this takes quite a long time and lot of effort - even to be part of a team building a product like this. Even if we give you all the advice and guidance you've asked for, at this stage as a non-programmer you probably won't understand what to do with 90% of it. To reach the top of the tallest mountain you first need to learn to climb a hummock - but instead you seem to have arrived at Everest base camp still wearing your flip-flops, and not knowing what gear is required for the climb, or how tall the mountain really is. I'm sorry if this sounds harsh but this is the reality, and hopefully this will save you a lot of disapointment in the short-term.

items.title: Don't give up, you are your own worst enemy, the whole AI thing is just a fad. When problems with th…
items.text: Don't give up, you are your own worst enemy, the whole AI thing is just a fad. When problems with that code start to “pop up,” they’ll realize it takes longer to fix them than it would have to do it “manually” (there are tons of companies that jumped on the “bandwagon” and are already jumping off). Worrying about the long-term future as an effective way to waste your time, as of today, we have nothing that we can describe as “artificial intelligence” (setting aside the fact that a certain type of program is grouped under that term, but it’s not a descriptive name, we could almost say it’s a “marketing” term). Now, this doesn’t mean we won’t have it tomorrow (as unlikely as that may seem, because we haven’t even taken the first step, no one knows what advances there will be in the future), better to focus on “sticking to the plan.”

items.title: You're reassigning source_folder inside your loop, so after the first .jpg file, source_folder is no…
items.text: You're reassigning source_folder inside your loop, so after the first .jpg file, source_folder is no longer the Downloads directory - its now the path to the first file you found. Use a different variable name inside the loop: import shutil import os home = '/home/mis1nput' source_folder = home + '/Downloads/' for filename in os.listdir(source_folder): if filename.endswith('.jpg'): # Use a NEW variable name,so that you dont overwrite source_folder source_path = os.path.join(source_folder, filename) destination_path = os.path.join(home + '/Pictures', filename) shutil.move(source_path, destination_path) additionally you might want to create destination folder if it doesn't exist:

items.title: First on the first iteration the source_folder becomes the file path, so on the second iteration the…
items.text: First on the first iteration the source_folder becomes the file path, so on the second iteration the source_folder is not a folder. source_folder = os.path.join(source_folder, filename)

items.title: I saw that : NotADirectoryError: [Errno 20] Not a directory: '/home/mis1nput/Downloads/image000000.j…
items.text: I saw that : NotADirectoryError: [Errno 20] Not a directory: '/home/mis1nput/Downloads/image000000.jpg/bagels12.jpg' -> '/home/mis1nput/Pictures/bagels12.jpg' Which means the compiler think you try to copy a .jpg in a jpg (which is not possible). Then the : shutil.move(source_folder,destination_folder) Should be in the loop, for each file Which means you must have : source_file = os.path.join(source_folder, filename) shutil.move(source_file, destination_folder) in your loop and your condition instead of source_folder = os.path.join(source_folder, filename) destination_folder = os.path.join(destination_folder, filename) shutil.move(source_folder,destination_folder) I tried it myself and it seems to work, let me know if it worked out for yourself

items.title: There's no (or little) commonality between web sites. If you're just looking to scrape static data y…
items.text: There's no (or little) commonality between web sites. If you're just looking to scrape static data you might do better just to copy and paste manually. If you want to scrape dynamic data then expect to put significant effort into each separate web site. Bear in mind that sites with valuable data are implementing increasingly sophisticated defences against automated scraping that might make it impossible to scrape them at all. In short focus your question if you want some specific advice.

items.title: You can add .Net assemblies as a resource and in the ApplicationEvents.vb file add a handler to reso…
items.text: You can add .Net assemblies as a resource and in the ApplicationEvents.vb file add a handler to resolve them to those resources Namespace My ' The following events are available for MyApplication: ' Startup: Raised when the application starts, before the startup form is created. ' Shutdown: Raised after all application forms are closed. This event is not raised if the application terminates abnormally. ' UnhandledException: Raised if the application encounters an unhandled exception. ' StartupNextInstance: Raised when launching a single-instance application and the application is already active. ' NetworkAvailabilityChanged: Raised when the network connection is connected or disconnected. Partial Friend Class MyApplication Private Sub AppStart(ByVal sender As Object, ByVal e As Microsoft.VisualBasic.ApplicationServices.StartupEventArgs) Handles Me.Startup AddHandler AppDomain.CurrentDomain.AssemblyResolve, AddressOf ResolveAssemblies End Sub Private Function ResolveAssemblies(sender As Object, e As System.ResolveEventArgs) As Reflection.Assembly Dim desiredAssembly = New Reflection.AssemblyName(e.Name) If desiredAssembly.Name = "Ookii.Dialogs.WinForms" Then Return Reflection.Assembly.Load(My.Resources.Ookii_Dialogs_WinForms) 'replace with your assembly's resource name Else Return Nothing End If End Function End Class End Namespace It is important to note this only works for .Net dlls

items.title: There isn’t a single Cursor setting that will monitor anything you type into the integrated terminal…
items.text: There isn’t a single Cursor setting that will monitor anything you type into the integrated terminal and automatically edit your code until the script stops crashing. You can do this: make Cursor detect terminal errors as editor diagnostics (clickable file/line in Problems) drive a loop where the Agent (or the Cursor CLI agent) runs the command, reads the traceback, fixes, reruns Turn terminal tracebacks into Problems (so Cursor can “detect” them) Don’t run python main.py manually. Run it as a VS Code task with the Python problem matcher. Create .vscode/tasks.json : { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "Run main.py", "type": "shell", "command": "${command:python.interpreterPath}", "args": ["${workspaceFolder}/main.py"], "problemMatcher": "$python", "presentation": { "reveal": "always", "revealProblems": "onProblem" } } ] } Now run it via Command Palette -> “Tasks: Run Task” -> “Run main.py”.

items.title: That plan to start simple by first implementing a skeletal framework, has always worked very well fo…
items.text: That plan to start simple by first implementing a skeletal framework, has always worked very well for me. There is always some critical and unexpectedly complicated detail that I discover when filling out the skeletal framework. If it is too hard to deal with, I bypass that feature with a mockup and move on to other parts of the framework. I get back to the mockups later as my understanding of the design implications improves. For an unfamiliar area, I have found there will be no design that can pass without major revisions as I discover new information about the subject. Checkpoints and design revisions are just part of the planning process. There is no such thing as a perfect design and no point in spending much effort towards having one. The only really good designs are for things that are very similar to what you have already done, and probably several times before. That circumstance happens in some jobs and not in others.

items.title: The time to complete a task (e.g. implement/code a procedure) has always been dependent on the exper…
items.text: The time to complete a task (e.g. implement/code a procedure) has always been dependent on the experience and skill level of the person(s) performing the task. Planning is also one of those tasks. When tackling a new project I like to start simple by first implementing a skeletal framework, and then incrementally implement in steps that gets into the details. The planning is also incremental and always on-going. Early in my career I've been involved in projects/contracts where there was a well-defined planning phase that produced a detailed project design & schedule. But designs and schedules are never perfect and always require revisions. Maybe corporate heads need that regimen for large teams, but I prefer a more flexible scheme.

items.title: My current approach uses a Boolean search query Search in - what? Are you querying some external new…
items.text: My current approach uses a Boolean search query Search in - what? Are you querying some external news API, your own database that you imported news articles into? Something else? Is this supposed to search in one single field of text, that contains just "everything"? Are do you have any sort of structured meta data about the news articles available? How can I refine this logic to avoid irrelevant results while ensuring I don't miss niche geopolitical updates? How do you expect us to answer that? We don't know what makes a result "irrelevant" to you, and what "niche geopolitical updates" would be important to you. I need a way to categorize these results by country automatically. What information do you have available to base this on? Originating website domain/article URL? Anything else?

items.title: For best performance when filtering large collections you should avoid linear comparisons, currently…
items.text: For best performance when filtering large collections you should avoid linear comparisons, currently you're looping entirely the $inventory collection for every item in $hostnames . Instead, use a hash-based lookup with a Hashset<string> for your $hostnames and a classic foreach loop with an if condition for filtering. You should also avoid += on an array, instead output from the loop and assign the loop expression to a variable, see array addition . Here is how the code would look: $hostnames = [System.Collections.Generic.HashSet[string]]::new( [string[]] @('server1', 'server2', 'server3', '...', 'server10000'), [System.StringComparer]::OrdinalIgnoreCase) $inventory = @( @{ name = 'server1'; ip = '10.0.0.1' } @{ name = 'server2'; ip = '10.0.0.2' } @{ name = 'notinhostnames'; ip = '10.0.0.2' }) $invtoryObjectsInHostnames = foreach ($object in $inventory) { if ($hostnames.Contains($object.name)) { $object } } $invtoryObjectsInHostnames # Name Value # ---- ----- # ip 10.0.0.1 # name server1 # ip 10.0.0.2 # name server2 Another way of doing it that also involves hash-based lookup is a hash table were each key is the name of each object in $inventory then the filtering loop is done over $hostnames (this is similar to the approach you were going for except much more efficient): $hostnames = @('server1', 'server2', 'server3', '...', 'server10000') $inventory = @( @{ name = 'server1'; ip = '10.0.0.1' } @{ name = 'server2'; ip = '10.0.0.2' } @{ name = 'notinhostnames'; ip = '10.0.0.2' }) $map = @{} foreach ($object in $inventory) { $map[$object.name] = $object } $invtoryObjectsInHostnames = foreach ($item in $hostnames) { if ($map.ContainsKey($item)) { $map[$item] } }

items.title: Why would you setup 2 threads on 2 different CPU's anyway? You'll run into al kind of caching issues…
items.text: Why would you setup 2 threads on 2 different CPU's anyway? You'll run into al kind of caching issues, or you run into NUMA overhead. But as always with performance issues, have you actually measured what's going on (using a profiler) and check for what is the bottleneck (cache-misses, memory-access, branch-misses etc...)

items.title: when you update an atomic value the CPU has to make sure that caches on all of the cores have the sa…
items.text: when you update an atomic value the CPU has to make sure that caches on all of the cores have the same value, this takes some time. When you have multiple CPUs it's even worse, the synchronisation is much slower as the two CPUs have to cooperate over a much slower bus than the bus internal to the CPU. The i5-10600KF doesn't even support multiple CPUs (as far as I'm aware no non-Xeon Intel CPUs do) so I'm not sure how you've got that working but it's likely not helping with performance. https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/products/sku/199315/intel-core-i510600kf-processor-12m-cache-up-to-4-80-ghz/specifications.html

items.title: Preemptive multitasking with heavy multithreading is an intrinsically noisy environment to try and t…
items.text: Preemptive multitasking with heavy multithreading is an intrinsically noisy environment to try and take short time measurements in. Try increasing the load by a factor of ten and see if that is enough to make the timings more consistent. I generally aim for a timed benchmark that takes ~1s for a quick look see and 10s for improved accuracy. That's usually enough to tidy it up. Doing the same thing 5 times gives you a better feel for how the timings may vary (and if there is any cache/memory access warm up time).

items.title: You will get more representative numbers if you make the system do more work so that it takes a few …
items.text: You will get more representative numbers if you make the system do more work so that it takes a few seconds to process. It is very hard to measure small times reliably in a multi-threaded environment.

items.title: Thanks! I appreciate the advice. I did increase the load which upped the time enough that it seems t…
items.text: Thanks! I appreciate the advice. I did increase the load which upped the time enough that it seems to be stable (no false regressions yet). Thankfully my testing framework handles performing warmup for me so that was no a factor.

items.title: Unnecessarily using 64 bit instructions with legacy registers increases code size, decreasing instru…
items.text: Unnecessarily using 64 bit instructions with legacy registers increases code size, decreasing instruction decode and cache efficiency. With r8 - r15, choosing 32-bit or 64-bit instructions doesn't affect code size. Also, in my opinion, it makes the code clearer to use the proper size register, just as in C it makes the code clearer to use the proper type.

items.title: Duplicate of The advantages of using 32bit registers/instructions in x86-64 Also Why is default oper…
items.text: Duplicate of The advantages of using 32bit registers/instructions in x86-64 Also Why is default operand size 32 bits in 64 mode? (in terms of machine code, 64-bit operand-size needs a REX prefix.) BTW, if you didn't already know the answer to this, you're probably pretty far from beating compilers at making more efficient asm by hand than you could get from using C and looking at the asm output, and adjusting the source to try to make it better. (or C++ or Rust etc.) See also https://agner.org/optimize/ and https://stackoverflow.com/tags/x86/info

items.title: One advantage of using 64 bit registers for known to be 32 bit values is that you don't have to worr…
items.text: One advantage of using 64 bit registers for known to be 32 bit values is that you don't have to worry about overflows when adding or multiplying a pair of 32 bit values together. IDK but I'd hazard a guess that 64bit operations consume slightly more power when active but I have never tested it. BTW this question has a factual definitive answer for any given CPU so would have been better off asked as a normal question. I would worry much more about getting the right algorithm correctly implemented than than the minutiae of register allocation and size, as ever YMMV. Using a profile directed compiler can save a lot of pain and suffering if you are after the very best optimisation of hotspots for maximum performance with least effort. The only way to be absolutely sure is to implement it both ways and measure! Experiment always trumps theory when it comes to real world behaviour.

items.title: I am with Josh here: why would you need to read all these rows? It certainly won't be for a person h…
items.text: I am with Josh here: why would you need to read all these rows? It certainly won't be for a person having to manually read millions of rows. So, it's a process? And it must read again and again all these rows? (If it were a one-time query, lack of speed wouldn't bother you.) Why wouldn't that process know like 99% of the data from the last time it read the data and only request the missing 1%? All in all, something seems off with the approch. It shouldn't be necessary to read millions of complete rows from the same table again and again. This means, it is probably not the query you want to optimize, but the process.

items.title: If you are only counting statements, consider that INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements typically only wo…
items.text: If you are only counting statements, consider that INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements typically only work with one or a few rows at a time, with limited accesses to related tables (typically to validate foreign key constraints). In contrast, SELECT statements (searches, information retrieval for display, reporting, data extracts, etc.) may access hundreds, thousands, or millions of rows to gather the needed information for the results. In general, the cost of defining and maintaining well-thought-out indexes results in savings many times over in SELECT query performance. So what constitutes well-thought-out indexes? First, most of your tables should have primary keys that are often also clustered indexes. Second, consider adding indexes to support most (but not necessarily all) foreign key columns. For example, the parent OrderId in an OrderDetail table should be indexed. This allows efficient access to the child rows (details) once a parent row (order) is selected. In the case of lookup tables, such as status code/name mappings, an FK index may not be needed, since usually the detail is retrieved before the status name lookup is performed. (That FK column might still be important in other compound indexes.) Next, take a look at the queries used to access your data. Identify what criteria each query uses to filter the initial data retrieval (before accessing other joined tables). This is where index creation becomes a craft. Don't just index each filter column separately. You need to define multi-column indexes that combine filter columns like dates, org IDs, and status IDs. (When writing queries, you should avoid filtering on things like StatusName or DepartmentName when you can lookup the corresponding StatusId and DepartmentId ahead of time and filter on those columns.) If it makes sense, place the most limiting filter column first. For example, if a query might typically look for "this month's sales for the entire eastern region", consider placing the SaleDate column before OrgId . If some of your tables contain "wide" tables (kitchen sink tables with many many columns), and you have queries that look up rows only to retrieve one or a few column values, consider adding covering indexes that have INCLUDE 'd columns to satisfy data retrieval requirements without having to access and retrieve each entire row. This can save a lot of I/O and reduce displacement of other valuable data cached in memory. Lastly, beware of recommended indexes shown in execution plans. Don't just blindly accept those recommendations, as this can lead to index bloat . Yes, you should examine them, but look for what is minimally needed to efficiently look-up the data, narrowing the results down to a manageable number or rows. It's okay to defer some minor filter conditions until after the actual row data is retrieved. This is a judgement call where you want indexes that yield significant performance improvements, but should avoid overly complicated indexes that have diminishing returns. Well, one more point: When in doubt, test your indexes using lots of data to see which ones actually improve performance when present vs absent. And by "lots of data", don't skimp. If your database is expected to have millions of rows, don't test using just a few thousand rows. Also, don't test using today's row counts. Test using row counts expected 5 or 10 years from now. Use a data generator if needed, and try to distribute data reasonably close to real world conditions. Opinions may differ, but this is my indexing philosophy.

items.title: The next BOMB number is **n = 8009**, with **V = 19**. --- **Why brute-force fails** For n = 270, `2…
items.text: The next BOMB number is **n = 8009**, with **V = 19**. --- **Why brute-force fails** For n = 270, `2^n + 3` already has ~82 decimal digits. `fordiv` must enumerate all divisors, which requires complete factorisation. By n = 8009 the number has ~2,413 digits — infeasible within any reasonable time. --- **The approach** To beat V = 14 we need a divisor *d* of `2^n + 3` with `v₂(d+9) ≥ 15`, i.e. `d ≡ −9 (mod 2^15)`. For n ≥ 15, `2^n + 3 ≡ 3 (mod 32768)`. So instead of factoring the whole number, we: 1. Trial-divide `2^n + 3` by small primes using modular exponentiation: `pow(2, n, p) == (-3) % p`. 2. Collect their residues mod 32768. 3. Check whether any **subset product** of those residues equals 32759 (≡ −9 mod 32768), via a DP over Z/32768Z. This never requires touching the ~2000-digit number directly. --- **The witness at n = 8009** `2^8009 + 3` is divisible by 5, 7, 947, and 8431 (each verifiable instantly): pow(2, 8009, 5) == 2 → 2 + 3 = 5 ≡ 0 (mod 5) ✓ pow(2, 8009, 7) == 4 → 4 + 3 = 7 ≡ 0 (mod 7) ✓ pow(2, 8009, 947) == 944 → 944 + 3 ≡ 0 (mod 947) ✓ pow(2, 8009, 8431) == 8428→ 8428 + 3 ≡ 0 (mod 8431)✓ Their product is the witness divisor: d = 5 × 7 × 947 × 8431 = 279,445,495 Check: d mod 32768 = 32759 ✓ (≡ −9 mod 2^15) d + 9 = 279,445,504 = 2^19 × 533 v₂(d + 9) = 19 ✓ (beats record of 14) --- **Verify in PARI-GP (runs in under a second)** d = 5\*7\*947\*8431; valuation(d+9, 2) \\\\ → 19 Mod(2,5)^8009 + 3 \\\\ → Mod(0,5) Mod(2,7)^8009 + 3 \\\\ → Mod(0,7) Mod(2,947)^8009 + 3 \\\\ → Mod(0,947) Mod(2,8431)^8009 + 3 \\\\ → Mod(0,8431) --- **Updated sequence** n V witness divisor 1 1 2 4 13 6 22 8 33 10 248 12 269 14 **8009** **19** **5 × 7 × 947 × 8431** Note: V jumps from 14 to 19 — the record is the maximum over *all* divisors, and at n = 8009 the best divisor happens to give 19, not 16.

items.title: I think we can use znlog to check values of n where 2^v*(6l±1)-9 divides 2^n+3, for(l=1,10^4,for(k=1…
items.text: I think we can use znlog to check values of n where 2^v*(6l±1)-9 divides 2^n+3, for(l=1,10^4,for(k=14,20,N=2^k*(6*l+1)-9;Z=znlog(Mod(-3,N),Mod(2,N));if(Z!=[],if(Z<10^4,print("2^",k,"*",6*l+1,"-9 / 2^",Z,"+3"))))) output: 2^14*55-9 / 2^2549+3 2^18*871-9 / 2^8665+3 for(l=1,10^4,for(k=14,20,N=2^k*(6*l-1)-9;Z=znlog(Mod(-3,N),Mod(2,N));if(Z!=[],if(Z<10^4,print("2^",k,"*",6*l-1,"-9 / 2^",Z,"+3"))))) output: 2^19*533-9 / 2^8009+3 Complexity: O(log d) via discrete log with n<10⁴ and 14≤v≤20. But the znlog method proves that ψ(2549)≥14,ψ(8665)≥18 and ψ(8009)≥19 where ψ(n) = max { v₂(d+9) : d ∣ 2ⁿ + 3 } and checking whether we found : the actual value of ψ requires checking ALL divisors of 2⁸⁰⁰⁹+3 which is hard!

items.title: I ended up using cliclick to create a .sh executable. If anyone else needs a simple autoclicker, the…
items.text: I ended up using cliclick to create a .sh executable. If anyone else needs a simple autoclicker, the script goes like this: while true; do cliclick c:. sleep $(awk -v min=0.5 -v max=1 'BEGIN { srand(); print min + rand() \* (max - min) }') cliclick c:. sleep $(awk -v min=3 -v max=4 'BEGIN { srand(); print min + rand() \* (max - min) }') done

items.title: In System Events, the click command can't simulate a mouse click at the mouse's current location. Ap…
items.text: In System Events, the click command can't simulate a mouse click at the mouse's current location. AppleScript isn't designed perform an actual act of 'clicking'; instead, AppleScript sends an Apple Event to the system or a process telling it that a click has been performed, so you have to spell out the details of this 'virtual' click, either by specifying specific global screen coordinates, e.g,: tell application "System Events" click at {384, 62} end tell or targeting a particular element of a process, e.g.: tell application "System Events" tell process "My Process" tell window 1 click button 3 end tell end tell end tell Making AppleScript perform direct clicks at the current mouse coordinates calls for other tools. You can use AppleScript ObjC to get the current mouse coordinates, or install a third party unix tool (google recommends 'cliclick') and call it from AppleScript's do shell script command.

items.title: It isn't completely clear what logic is used in the Portal to determine what option is checked, howe…
items.text: It isn't completely clear what logic is used in the Portal to determine what option is checked, however here is an estimation, you can get the User consent settings by querying Get authorizationPolicy and inspecting the permissionGrantPoliciesAssigned property from defaultUserRolePermissions : Connect-MgGraph -Scopes Policy.Read.All $uri = 'v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy?$select=defaultUserRolePermissions' $req = Invoke-MgGraphRequest GET $uri $req.defaultUserRolePermissions.permissionGrantPoliciesAssigned Note in the details of defaultUserRolePermissions : Indicates if user consent to apps is allowed, and if it is, which permission to grant consent and which app consent policy (permissionGrantPolicy) govern the permission for users to grant consent. Value should be in the format managePermissionGrantsForSelf.{id} , where {id} is the id of a built-in or custom app consent policy . An empty list indicates user consent to apps is disabled. Then checking the Built-in consent policies there is a warning that says: microsoft-user-default-recommended and microsoft-user-default-allow-consent-apps are a Microsoft managed policies. The conditions included in the policies are automatically updated based on Microsoft's latest security recommendations for end-user consent. So my estimation is: $policies = $req.defaultUserRolePermissions.permissionGrantPoliciesAssigned if ($policies.Where{ $_ -match '^managePermissionGrantsForSelf\.' }.Count -eq 0) { # this means that the first option is checked } elseif ($policies -match '\.microsoft-user-default-(?:recommended|allow-consent-apps)') { # this means that the last option is checked } else { # the middle option is checked } In my case, I have the middle option checked and if I inspect the array of permissionGrantPoliciesAssigned I have ManagePermissionGrantsForSelf.microsoft-user-default-low which according to the docs is: microsoft-user-default-low : All low risk permissions consentable by member type users by default.

items.title: First, make a backup and make sure you can restore it in case you fat-finger something. Even better,…
items.text: First, make a backup and make sure you can restore it in case you fat-finger something. Even better, try out all these optimizations on a staging site before using them on a live site. I wrote a free non-monetized plugin a few years ago to overcome the indexing efficiency difficulties caused by the need to support legacy versions of the MySql DBMS. It is seeing some success. https://wordpress.org/plugins/index-wp-mysql-for-speed/ It gets rid of the notorious prefix indexes that slow things down, especially in the meta tables. There are several database-cleaning plugins. I like Younes JFR’S Advanced Database Cleaner. It can scrub out a lot of orphaned cruft and old post revisions. https://wordpress.org/plugins/advanced-database-cleaner/ Tables left behind by plugins you uninstalled? Here’s what I do. Open a support topic with the developer. We’re supposed to drop tables we created on uninstall. When we don’t it’s a defect. Use a SQL client (phpmyadmin) to say RENAME TABLE wp_old_plugin_table TO drop_me_old_plugin_table Then, a month later if everything is working right I DROP the table. If something breaks and the old table is still needed it’s easy to rename it back. Another thing to consider: add a persistent object cache to reduce the database workload. And ask your hosting provider to set these two directives in php.ini, to make php run WordPress more efficiently. opcache.memory_utilization=256 opcache.interned_strings_buffer=24

items.title: Which latency optimizations are generally considered low-risk from a correctness perspective? Latenc…
items.text: Which latency optimizations are generally considered low-risk from a correctness perspective? Latency can be caused by network delays, queuing on the server side or processing time on the server side. To reduce network delays, you could use faster network technology. Or you could use a network protocol with less overhead. But the latter can introduce correctness problems. Special networking hardware can be used to accelerate message handling on network level. Server queuing is determined by the number of parallel servers, server threads or processes. A finite queue might overflow and thus lead to errors. The processing time on the server side is determined by the algorithms and by the implementation technology (eg compiled vs interpreted). Limited throughput of a database might also determine the latency. One optimization is to collect message acknowledgements. The sender assumes that the message is received and processed correctly. It does not wait for the reply but receives an aggregated acknowledgement later on. It depends on the nature of messages if this is a viable option. In a sessionless architecture, latencies can be reduced by adding more server nodes. But the data on the server side has to be kept consistent. Another aspect is maintenance. Imagine a scenario with millions of messages distributed across dozens of servers. How can such an environment be safely backed-up, restored, monitored or updated? A messaging platform might be needed to master these tasks. Which optimizations most often introduce correctness bugs? Connectionless Shoot and forget protocols like UDP typically lead to dropped messages causing potential inconsistencies. Protocols with session control and exactly-once-in-order capabilities avoid omissions and alterations in the order of transmission at the cost of decreased performance. They take care of automatic retransmission of faulty messages. Commercial systems typically require transactional correctness. A transaction might consist of a number of messages which either have to be processed completely or not processed at all. What safeguards or validation techniques are commonly used to ensure correctness is preserved? On network level, typical safeguards are checksums (the simplest form of a digital signature) and sequence numbers. They help to detect changed message contents, message omissions/duplicates and message sequence alterations. TCP ensures correct order and message integrity. Higher level of integrity is achieved by TLS (Transport Layer Security). TLS is used in many service-oriented architectures. On server level, errors may be caused by overloads, internal server errors, configuration errors, data inconsistencies, faults within server hardware/software. To guard against such faults, one can introduce redundancy and plausibility checking. Transactional correctness often is ensured by the underlying database. If a transaction is not completed sucessfully, it is rolled back either manually or automatically.

items.title: In fish-shell, all variables are indeed true lists. They are not stored as delimited strings. That s…
items.text: In fish-shell, all variables are indeed true lists. They are not stored as delimited strings. That said, it is easy to confuse a multi-item list, and a single-item list where the single entry contains spaces. fish provides set --show var_name for this purpose. $ set -l a aa ab ac ad $ echo $a[2] ab This creates a variable named "a" with four items: "aa", "ab", "ac", "ad". We can check that with set --show a : $ set --show a $a: set in local scope, unexported, with 4 elements $a[1]: |aa| $a[2]: |ab| $a[3]: |ac| $a[4]: |ad| Now when we move to this: $ set a (echo $a | sed 's/a/z/g') $ echo $a zz zb zc zd The variable "a" is a list with one item "zz zb zc zd". This item contains spaces: $ set --show a $a: set in local scope, unexported, with 1 elements $a[1]: |zz zb zc zd| This is because the command substitution (the parenthesized bit) splits on newlines, not spaces. We can split on spaces if we want, via string split , giving us a list again: $ set a (echo $a | sed 's/a/z/g' | string split ' ') $ set --show a $a: set in local scope, unexported, with 4 elements $a[1]: |zz| $a[2]: |zb| $a[3]: |zc| $a[4]: |zd| As a rule, prefer to use a list of items rather than a space-delimited single-item list, because it properly handles filenames with spaces.

items.title: The approach you are following is the correct one when using the APIs directly. If you want to trigg…
items.text: The approach you are following is the correct one when using the APIs directly. If you want to trigger a pre-indexing task, such as the one required to populate Collections, it is expected that you call the Ingestion API endpoints directly. A few clarifications: The API client’s chunkedPush method does not introduce any special indexing behavior by itself. It simply splits your records into batches, 1,000 records by default, and sends each batch as a push request. Unfortunately, it is not possible to use saveObjects first and then trigger the Collections task afterward, even if you keep track of the related taskId . Since this is a pre-indexing transformation, it has to run before the records are indexed. Otherwise, you risk having data indexed in the live indices without the proper Collections tags applied. If you move this job to an SDK-based implementation (as Algolia recommends), the recommended approach is: Use saveObjectsWithTransformation to add new records or overwrite existing ones. Use partialUpdateObjectsWithTransformation for incremental updates when only some attributes of existing records need to change. Use replaceAllObjectsWithTransformation for full reindexing. This method creates a temporary index for you and swaps it with the live index once indexing is complete, which is effectively the same pattern you are implementing manually today. Also worth noting about these methods: They automatically apply the index’s Collections task, as long as there is no other pre-indexing connector configured on that index. Under the hood, they use chunkedPush to split records into batches when necessary. Batch size can be configured with the WithBatchSize option. They can be made synchronous with the WithWaitForTasks option. TL;DR: if you stay on direct API calls, your current model is the correct one. If you move to an SDK, replaceAllObjectsWithTransformation is the cleanest option for your daily full reindex flow.

items.title: Regular expression; The point here is that matching on line numbers, and fixed strings, does not gen…
items.text: Regular expression; The point here is that matching on line numbers, and fixed strings, does not generalize. There is a principle in software engineering that apples here; 'DRY' (a.k.a. Don't Repeat Yourself) to the sed command; esentially your lead system admin want's you to figure out how to use one command to do many (e.g., 3) things. As for a advice: Read up on what does '^$' mean in regular expression (The answer is in the manpage for re_format man re_format ) and then consider what differs between ^# and ^[^#] . That will get your head around what's going on. As for your question: how would you grep the first comment, last comment, and then anything in between those comments delete Also, I apologise to your lead system admin for just answering: Try a single awk instead of multiple grep and sed stuff (albeit there is a nasty way to use a single sed, I'd advise against it): e.g.: START='# begin-block' END='# end-block' awk -v S="$START" -v E="$END" ' $0 == S { in=1; next } $0 == E && in { in=0; next } !in ' file And, my final advice is to understand why this awk strategy is DRY, and helpful to your scenario. (as a beginner, you can have an LLM explain this to you) Other useful concepts that may have been overlooked (e.g., for a beginner): delimiters (e.g., why patterns instead of lines) file parsing (e.g., why reach for awk) regular expression (namely what Extended RE can do rather than just how ) coupling - Namely: bash pipes and pipeline type design (in the context of system's engineering) The key differences between the tools: awk, ast-grep, grep, and sed

items.title: Even when using .select(), SharePoint may still internally process large fields like multi-line text…
items.text: Even when using .select(), SharePoint may still internally process large fields like multi-line text (especially if they are part of the default view or indexed poorly). This impacts performance significantly. Use $select + $top + Pagination Limit the number of items per request and process in batches: const items = await sp.web.lists.getByTitle("My List Data").items.select("Id", "Title") // only required fields.top(100)(); // batch size Then use pagination: let allItems: any[] = []; let pagedItems = await sp.web.lists.getByTitle("My List Data").items.top(100)(); while (pagedItems.hasNext) { allItems = allItems.concat(pagedItems.results); pagedItems = await pagedItems.getNext(); } console.log("allItems:", allItems);

items.title: And they probably have their ecosystem spread out geographically with different regions of the world…
items.text: And they probably have their ecosystem spread out geographically with different regions of the world having their own data storage. Something like Google or Facebook serve "millions of requests per second". Probably combined. The naive answer to your question is "do what Google does" and know that it takes a Google-sized organization to achieve this.

items.title: Select action on the input Array with only Title column (Group by column) - item()?['Title'] Compose…
items.text: Select action on the input Array with only Title column (Group by column) - item()?['Title'] Compose action with Union function on output of Select action - union(body('Select:_Get_Control_Numbers'),body('Select:_Get_Control_Numbers')) 3. Initialize a new Array variable - vControlAnwers 4. Apply to each loop on output of above Compose action. Include next 4 actions in this loop. 4.1 Filter array action with input of initial Array and query as array Title column is equal to Apply to each Title column. item()?['Title'] is equal to items('Apply_to_each:_Control_Answers')?['Title'] 4.2 Select action on body of Filter array and map field is Answer column (concatenate column) - item()?['Answer'] 4.3 Join action with body of above Select and join with new line - decodeUriComponent('%0A') 4.4 Append to new array variable (vControlAnswers) with Title column from Apply to each item and Answer column from body of above Join action - { "Title": "items('Apply_to_each:_Control_Answers')?['Title']", "Answer": body('Join:_Control_Answers') }
